So in some ways you didn't like create the vision but you are in some ways expected to execute it or take a piece of it and do it. And I find that sometimes what new managers don't pay attention to enough is what is their true belief. Like they feel like they might have to be like a soldier. So they just get orders and they have to execute it. But it really makes a difference if they themselves have gone through the work of thinking through — wait why are we doing this? Do I believe this strategy? Does it make sense or not? And if it doesn't make sense to go and actually have the conversation with, you know, their manager or whoever else — just so they can get to alignment on like I really believe in what I'm doing. Because if you don't really believe in what you're doing and or you're just kind of parroting the thing that got passed through the organization, it's very hard for you to then be able to help other people see what that magic is or to be actually really effective as a person who can hold that vision and that purpose.

So, I just think you have to really check in with yourself on like, wait, I know we're told to do this and this is what we have to do, but how do we really feel about it? Because if you don't feel good about it, then it's not going to be very likely that the project's going to succeed. **Lenny Rachitsky:** This is such a classic challenge of managers — is getting things done that you don't really agree with. I just — this is not a good idea — but I need to have a brave face and not make it sound like I'm just being told what to do and I'm just reporting orders. I don't believe in this. Like you don't want to do that. You become a terrible unsuccessful manager and people lose trust in you. What's your advice to folks that are in that place of just how to find that balance?

**Julie Zhuo:** So, I think first if you feel that way, you got to actually find a way to get it out and engage in dialogue. So, if you're like, I think my manager told me to do this, I think it's a terrible idea — you got to talk to your manager about it or you got to talk to the CEO or whoever — and feel because once you engage in a dialogue, what will often happen is you'll learn more. Like you'll have new information, you'll have new assumptions and maybe you'll have influenced a project in some manner. But often the more you can learn about — okay why did some other smart people feel like we should do this — and what parts of it do I believe and what parts am I more skeptical about — like you can probably decompose it from a blanket it's good or bad to like okay there's this hypothesis, this is a hypothesis, this is a hypothesis. I might kind of believe this one. The reason I don't like the proposal — I don't believe this particular hypothesis but I believe these other ones right.

And so when you can start to get one level deeper into breaking it down into a set of assumptions that makes it much easier because then you'll likely find something that you do kind of resonate with and you might be able to then steer things like okay that hypothesis — you can disagree and commit — but now we can be very specific, we can isolate the thing. And what we can also often do is like okay the reason I didn't like this proposal is because I believe that this assumption is wrong, right? So — and they come up with like a really stupid example — but you know, your suggestion is I know we have a great idea. We're going to go and put a lemonade stand on every block. And my core assumption is people do not like lemonade. That's not the hot beverage right now. And so I therefore think this is a stupid plan. But if I talk to you about it and you're like, "No, no, this is the core assumption we disagree on." Likely what starts to unfold is like, "Well, let's just — can we get some data? Can we get some information? Like can we just — is there a quicker way to validate whether people like lemonade? Perhaps we should just test it in one market before we go and open up the lemonade stands across the entire 50 states."

And so what happens is we can likely get to the actual specific area and come up with something. And then if I have to now share with my team — you know, we're going to try this hypothesis. And so for example, if my kids say something like uh going outside is boring or taking a walk is boring or doing something is boring. My general response will be — well it's because you're not seeing the infinity that's in that direction. So even for example something really mundane like staring at a blank wall.

I think that you can make that actually deeply deeply interesting because you can use that as an opportunity to go into your own mind and to figure out like how you can make time pass or you can meditate on the existence or meditate on your breath or just be grateful for the purpose of being alive and like — two people, right? One person you can say sit in front of a wall for an hour and they will — like my kid — super complain and be like this is the worst thing ever. But you can put somebody else like a monk and they'll have a wonderful experience. And so it's not really about the environment or the wall. It's really about how we see it and whether we can find the thing that is deep and rich and infinite in that direction.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Wow. These are some deep answers. This is very uh I don't know — Buddhist — very mindfulness oriented. I did a retreat once and their advice was just yeah anytime you're bored just notice all the things that are going on around you like what does your seat feel like right now, what does the air feel like, you know, what are you hearing right now. And it's exactly what you're saying. There's infinite things to pay attention to and keep you interested. It's hard to actually do that for a long time and practice.

**Julie Zhuo:** That's why it's a practice. But I repeat that to myself because oftentimes if I have a bad experience I'm feeling a certain day. It helps me to realize that like it's often probably in my head. Like it's because I haven't gained the skills to be able to see the richness and infinity in that and that's — so I can maybe work on that and that feels better than feeling like oh I'm a victim of my circumstances. Like this thing happened to me and that's so awful but now I'm powerless. I can't do anything about — like that to me is a worse feeling than the alternative which is I just don't have the skill yet. I can recognize it for what it is. I don't have the skill yet, but I can grow. I can maybe get better at it. There is a person out there who had the same situation as me and feels much more positively than I do. And don't I want to be more like that person?

**Lenny Rachitsky:** That's such a beautiful circle back to our very first episode, which a lot of it was on imposter syndrome and overcoming that and your story there. So, I love that. That's a maybe a way to close this conversation. But before we do that and before we get to our very exciting lightning round, is there anything else that you wanted to mention or share or double down on that we've talked about?

**Julie Zhuo:** I just want to say thank you. Honestly, I'm so inspired by the work that you do. You know, we've known each other for quite a while. Um, and I just think from the very first idea that you had for this newsletter, for the podcast, it's been incredible and I think the world gets so much from it. I'm sure you hear that a lot, but I am very grateful.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Well, I really appreciate that and I say this every time we do a chat is just uh this wouldn't have been possible without you, Julie. Uh I was inspired by your longtime newsletter, The Looking Glass. That's essentially my idea was what if I do this for product and uh I started on Medium just like you did and then I moved to Substack and then it's like what if I charge for this and then that worked and then I'm like what if I do a podcast and then that worked but it all began with your your concept. So thank you truly.

**Julie Zhuo:** Yeah, and I think you do it with so much kindness and curiosity as you always have. So, I love that.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** That's just who I am.

**Julie Zhuo:** Well, with that, we have reached our very exciting lightning round. I've got five questions for you. Are you ready?

**Lenny Rachitsky:** I'm ready.

**Julie Zhuo:** What are two or three books that you find yourself recommending most to other people?

**Julie Zhuo:** The first is Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance. I absolutely love that book. It's beautifully written. It's so deep. Um my whole philosophy around quality — it really comes a lot of it comes from that book. The idea and even all the stuff that we talked about — change — what does it mean to be at that forefront of change and dynamic quality. I think he just talks about it so beautifully and so masterfully in that book. So old classic but I try to reread it every few years or so.

Second is Conscious Business. It is my favorite management book. It's a little bit of a sleeper hit because I actually end up recommending this one far more than my own book.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Oh wow.

**Julie Zhuo:** I read this one after I wrote my book and I always tell people that if I read it before, I'm not sure I would have written my book because I would have been like conscious business is really the book that um really really so much resonates. And I think what — and many of the things I talked about — this idea of win-win, the idea of like being a player, not a victim, um and how to think about work, not just it's a job, but like how do you really think about aligning it with your own personal values and what you want to do in the world. I think that this book really speaks to that so beautifully. It is also very tactical. It's got a lot of really wonderful examples. Um, I will tell people the cover isn't very attractive and I think that if you judge a book by its cover, like this seems very corporate-y. The title also seems like kind of like what — conscious business — and the first chapter is a little bit more technical, but if you just could get past it and get into chapter 2 and you start, you know, with examples of the soccer team — it's just like the best management book.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** That is good uh good advice to get people over the hump when they look for it and they're like, "Okay, okay, I'm gonna stick with it."

**Julie Zhuo:** Yes. And okay, third book. Um, I love the uh the book Good Inside by Dr. Becky. It's a parenting book and it's a very wildly popular parenting book. So, I really recommend it to all parents. But I also think it's just a wonderful book for thinking about relationships and because parenting is — it's like a very very deep and intense relationship and interaction that you have with another human being. And there's so many things that I read in parenting books including Good Inside by Dr. Becky that I think like could just as well have been like a management or a team leadership book.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Uh I am thinking about trying to ask Dr. Becky to come on the podcast. I feel like there could be a lot of synergy exactly for that reason.

**Julie Zhuo:** And she uses this term sturdy which — I probably got it — I I mean I think she talks a lot about sturdiness and that just incepted right in here.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Yes. Yeah. Her whole thing is creating — being a sturdy parent. Strong but flexible I imagine. Uh yeah, I love I love her and I love her stuff. I watch all her videos on TikTok and Emily Oster. Okay, next question. Uh is there a movie or TV show you enjoyed?

**Julie Zhuo:** I have not watched anything — I have no good answer for you. I think the only thing I watched this year was a rewatch of La La Land, which I do truly love.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** So delightful. Okay. Is there a product you recently discovered that you really love?

**Julie Zhuo:** I don't think there's anything too new. I love Granola. I love Replit. I've used all of the different coding apps. Cursor is is big on me for now. Um, uh, I just got a Madic robot. I think that's been really delightful so far. At least the setup. I haven't used it like a long long term, but the setup, the way that it worked, the fact that it had little stickers and you could make it into a dog or a cat was like a wonderful experience.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** The Madic robot — I'll bubble link to it. I am also a huge fan. I'm not an investor. That's uh essentially Waymo meets Roomba for folks that don't know anything about it. It's like a very sophisticated robot vacuum built by like AI vision people.

**Julie Zhuo:** Oh, I just thought of one more as well. Uh the Limitless pendant.

So disclaimer, I am an uh a small investor in Limitless. But uh what I love about it is that um so okay, it's a pendant. You wear it and it just records everything that's going on and later it summarizes things and it gives you feedback. And I don't usually wear it out because I find that maybe other people feel like awkward that I'm like recording everything. I usually try to get people's permission. But I do wear it at home when I'm with my kids. And one of the best things that the pendant does is it gives me feedback on parenting.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** What?

**Julie Zhuo:** Like automatically or do you throw it into ChatGPT?

No, it will — it's automatic — like there's an app and it will sometimes notify me or if I check it, it'll — or I can also engage with like ask it. Um, but what it does is essentially it's like Granola but like for your life in terms of capturing everything, summarizing it and then giving you tips and feedback. And it said things like, "Hey, there was that time you were talking about the game and you cut your kid off a lot and maybe next time think about letting them speak fully and listening better."

**Lenny Rachitsky:** The app itself natively does that.

**Julie Zhuo:** Yeah. I did not know that because I have one. I haven't used it much recently. That is incredible. Yeah. I wonder if it gives you relationship advice too if you're talking to your partner. I wonder how it even knows.

**Julie Zhuo:** Um it's so — it was — it did a pretty good job of inferring you know that or I think it's a person too, but it was kind of eye opening for me.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Incredible. So there's a recent episode of the How I AI podcast — our sister podcast — where somebody uh wears that in their meetings with their CEO and automatically turns what they're asking for into a prototype from the meeting notes and then sales teams can start showing it to people to see if they're interested. How about that?

**Julie Zhuo:** That's awesome. That is super cool.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Holy moly. Okay, I'll keep going. Uh do you have a favorite life motto that you find yourself repeating to yourself, sharing with others?

**Julie Zhuo:** I like make it happen. Just a reminder that at the end of the day we can do a lot — we can have a lot of motion. Maybe this is another one that I really like. Um I think about this poster. It used to be a poster at Facebook that says don't mistake motion for progress. So there's this idea of like be the change you want to be in the world. I guess there's all other ways of saying the same thing which is like you know — I can do things. We can all do things. We have better and better tools to go out there and make things happen. Make it happen.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Like the common meme on Twitter, you can just do things.

**Julie Zhuo:** Yes.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Final question. I like to ask this question of folks that are really deep in AI and been working with AI and kind of getting a sense of where things are going. Is there something that you teach your kids or teaching your kids — think about encouraging them to learn — knowing that AI is going to be a big part of their life?

**Julie Zhuo:** Emotional regulation is still really, really, really important. That's probably the thing that I think about the most in terms of what I want my kids to learn. I want my kids to be able to introspect, to have a better understanding of where their state of mind is because we're still human. We still have the same hardware that humans have had for thousands of years. And that's not changing even as the tools and the environment around us change.

And so I feel that you have to really understand yourself and you have to understand what's going on for you and where you are biased and where you are not because AI can make it — and this is my great fear — is that it makes things so much more comfortable. And I have this great fear that this has been the trajectory that we've been on with technology right — this is again going back to like every strength is a weakness — technology makes things a lot easier, that's why we invent. Like we're always — the human race has always been about trying to better our circumstances and in some ways control our destiny like control our future. But at the same time, all of that control gets to a point where we have so many shortcuts in our lives and you can shortcut a lot of things. I can shortcut relationships. You can shortcut hard feelings because now you can just watch TikTok instead of actually dealing with the very difficult emotion or tension that you had with a colleague or with your partner or with your children. And AI makes it even I think more attractive because now there's a person who can — or there's a thing that can be very very personalized — and if you're like I want a distraction I want to do something you got that. But how do we actually still learn to sit with what is our true biology that's not changing and how do we continue to be the kind of people that want to take on the freedom of doing challenging things?

Because I find that if we don't do challenging things we just suffer — we suffer in a different way. And so to me, true freedom is you can pick the things that are hard and you can feel pride in becoming the thing that you want to be. It's not forced upon you. Like it's not for survival sake anymore, but you still have to pick. And I want to figure out for my children the fact that like it is really important to still find the challenge. Yes, you can use AI to do that, but really don't think about it as a shortcut tool. Um because if that's the case, I don't actually think that they're going to be able to become the kind of people they want to be in the world.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** What a beautiful way to end this conversation, Julie. It feels like this is just like some kind of huge milestone of this podcast, just like having you back three years later. It's like a I don't know, a chapter in the journey. Uh I appreciate you coming back. I appreciate you sharing all this wisdom with us. Two final questions. Where can folks find you online if they want to reach out and maybe chat about Sundial, maybe whatever else you're up to — and then how can listeners be useful to you?

**Julie Zhuo:** Well, I would love to work with people who are at companies building really cool things and want better answers to um how we build better. And so if you think your company would be interested in working with us at Sundial um and figuring out how do we make every single decision maker into their own expert analyst, please reach out. So that's one area. So, um, sundial at sundial.so. Uh, I am on X, so I've been tweeting a lot more, sharing, uh thoughts — you know, going back to that skill of practicing just share what's on your mind. But for the long form stuff, I have my blog, The Looking Glass. It's on Substack. Uh, I share articles and thoughts about AI, product building, leadership periodically.

And then, of course, I have my book — the a revised edition with two additional chapters. One is around managing remotely and the other one is around uh managing in a downturn or managing in change — difficult change scenarios. Um that will be coming out in two weeks time.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** It's going — the new content will be in the paperback. That's important — and I will send you a version of this when I get a copy myself, Lenny. But the paperback has a gradient type of cover. Um the hardback will eventually get the new content but it just takes a while to phase out from all of the different retailers. So, if you buy one, I cannot guarantee that it's going to have the new content, but certainly the Kindle and the paperback will have all of the new content.

**Julie Zhuo:** And then, so just for the publish date, because this might come out later, what's the date it's coming out? Just for folks.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** September 9th.

**Julie Zhuo:** Okay. Amazing. So, I think it'll be out by the time this is out, so go buy it. I imagine available Amazon, all your local retailers.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Yes. Yes.

**Julie Zhuo:** Amazing. Julie, thank you so much for being here.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Thank you so much, Lenny. This was so fun. I hope to be back in another three years or whatever the next chapter is.

**Julie Zhuo:** Hopefully sooner.

**Lenny Rachitsky:** Bye everyone.

**Julie Zhuo:** Bye.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** Julie，非常感谢你来到节目，欢迎回来。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Julie, thank you so much for being here and welcome back to the podcast.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 谢谢你，Lenny。我非常兴奋能来到这里。整整一周我都在期待这次对话。我非常喜欢你的播客，也很欣赏你从我们第一次对话以来把它发展到了什么样的高度。我超级期待今天能有一次有趣又有深度的聊天。你能相信我们的第一期节目已经是三年多前了吗？天哪。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Thank you, Lenny. I'm so excited to be here. I've been looking forward to this all week. I love your podcast. I love where you've taken it since our very first conversation, and I'm super excited to have a fun and engaging chat. Can you believe that first episode, the very first episode of this podcast was over three years ago at this point. Holy

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我不确定你那时候背景里有没有那个壁炉。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I'm not sure you had that fire in the background back then.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 说起来很好玩，不知道有多少人注意到了我一直保留的这个彩蛋。在第一个录制间里，我刚才还在回看那一期，我有一面搞笑的小镜子。我不记得第一期里有没有壁炉在镜子里映出来——因为镜子当时照到了一些奇怪的东西。所以我就在后来换过的每个录制间里都保留了这个壁炉。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So, funny enough, I don't know how many people have noticed this Easter egg that I've stuck with. Uh, in that first studio, I was just watching the episode. I had like this funny little mirror. I don't know if I had in the first episode with a fireplace that was showing up in that mirror because the mirror was showing something stupid. And so I've just kind of kept this fireplace across every studio I've moved across in my various places.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我还记得我们那次聊天的时候，视频还算是挺新的形式。你当时说会录视频，但重点其实是音频。现在我们已经进入视频时代了。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I even remember when we chatted video was like kind of a newer thing. You're like we'll record it but like it's really about the audio and now we moved into the video era.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 你说这话的时候我发现我的壁炉坏了，赶紧打开了。所以我们剪掉了一小段。那个壁炉是我给自己留的一个小彩蛋，我觉得从来没人发现过。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So as you were saying that I realized my fire was broken so I just had to turn that on. So we just cut a little piece. Uh yeah that fire was my little fun Easter egg for myself and I don't think anyone's ever realized this.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 很温馨，我很喜欢。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** It's very cozy. I love it.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 就是这个意思。我刚刚在看数据。从第一期到现在，这个播客已经超过两千万次下载了，快接近三千万了。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** That's the idea. Uh, I was actually just looking at the stats. So, since that first episode, this podcast has done over 20 million downloads. It's approaching 30 million downloads.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 真的很不可思议。我觉得这是对你的好奇心以及你对打造优秀产品这门手艺的热爱的见证——你把这些分享给了全世界。我和我的团队都在听你的播客、读你的通讯，我们经常分享你请过的那些嘉宾说过的精彩内容。所以，谢谢你做这些。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** It's really incredible. I think it is a testament to just your curiosity and how much you really care about the craft of building great products and sharing that with the world. And I know I listen to your podcast and read your newsletter. My team does. We're constantly sharing things from all the amazing speakers that you've had. So, thank you for doing this.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我的荣幸，真的很感谢。我们三年后再次聊天的原因是——你正在重新出版你那本了不起的书《The Making of a Manager》。我手上就有一本。你已经卖了超多本，上过我见过的每一个榜单。你正在出平装版，还增加了一些章节。首先想问你，回顾这本书的成功，你有什么感受？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Uh, my pleasure. I really appreciate that. So, the reason we are chatting again three years later is uh you're re-releasing your incredible book, The Making of a Manager. I've got it right here. Uh you've sold a bazillion copies. It's been on every list I've seen. Uh you're releasing the paperback version. You're adding some chapters. I guess first of all, just how do you feel on the reflecting back on the the success of this book?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 说实话，它超出了我的预期，所以我非常开心。我写这本书最大的动力是——我觉得如果我不得不写出这些东西，我可能会成为一个更好的管理者。这其实是很重要的一部分。因为思考和写作——我写博客很多年了，我知道当我真正坐下来把所有感受都写出来、给自己写信的时候，这真的很有帮助。所以这确实是我最初的一个重要动力。我当时希望这本书能出去卖一些，想着也许在像 Facebook 这样的硅谷高增长公司长大的人会产生共鸣。我完全没想到它会有这么广泛的影响力。很多人会告诉我："我以为只有我一个人有这种感觉，但这本书让我意识到这些都是很正常的感受。"那正是我多年来跌跌撞撞、觉得自己是冒充者时的感受。所以听到读者这么说，真的非常令人欣慰。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** It honestly went beyond my expectations. So, I'm super happy with it. My big motivation to write it was, I think, largely because I felt if I had to write this thing, I was likely going to become a better manager. And that was actually a huge part of it. Um, because thinking about and writing something, you know, I've been blogging for a long time and I know that part of my process is when I really sit down to try and put down everything I feel and write letters to myself, it really helps me. And so that was honestly a huge motivation. I hope that it would go out there and it would sell some books. I was thinking about that maybe for people who grew up in companies like mine like Facebook, you know, high-scale Silicon Valley, it might resonate. I couldn't have expected that it would have much wider reach than that. And um and that's been really awesome. And just how many people will tell me things like, I thought I was the only one who felt this way, but this book made me realize that, hey, these are very normal feelings. And that's certainly how I felt just stumbling through and feeling like an impostor for so many years. And so I'm it really is very gratifying to hear that from readers.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我觉得它就像是现代版的《High Output Management》。那是这个播客上被提到最多的书，而你的书感觉就像一个现代版本。我觉得那本老书在很多方面已经过时了，所以我能理解为什么大家这么喜欢你的书。这也是一个很好的引子，我想花点时间聊第一个话题——感觉作为管理者学到的很多技能，可以直接迁移到善用 AI 和 AI 工具上。我想跟你讨论几个趋势。第一个是，感觉未来每个人都会成为管理者，因为 agent 正在深度融入我们的工作流程。我们正在进入一个 agentic 社会，而管理者的技能恰好让你在使用 agent 时非常出色。你怎么看？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I feel like it's the modern-day High Output Management. It feels that's the book that's been mentioned most on this podcast. And it feels like this is just like a modern version. I feel like that older book is actually out of date in a lot of ways. So I can see why people are really drawn to it. And this is a great segue to the first area I want to spend some time on, which is it feels like a lot of the skills you learn as a manager translate to being really good with AI and using AI tools really well. And I want to talk through a few trends that uh I want to get your take on that relate to this general theme. The first is it feels like just everyone is going to become a manager in the near future because of agents being so integrated into our workflows. There's this agentic society that we're coming to and it feels like the same skills of being a manager make you really good working with agents. Uh just thoughts on that and where you think that's going to go.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我百分之百同意。在我看来，管理的本质就是拥有一个目标——你想完成某件事。你有一个北极星，你有一个愿景，你要弄清楚如何用手头的资源来实现它。传统上我们说管理中的资源就是人——招到合适的人才，确保团队有合适的技能组合。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I 100% believe that and agree with that which is that management is just about in my mind having an outcome. So you want to get something done. That's the thing. You have a northstar. You have a vision and you're just trying to figure out how to use the resources that you have to get that thing done. And typically when we talk about management in traditional settings, we talk about the resources being people and getting the right talent and making sure that you know you've got the — assemble the adventure. So you've got the right mix of skills that you need.

bilingual.zh: 第二个杠杆是目标——每个人都知道自己该用才华做什么吗？我们有目标吗？有使命感吗？第三个就是流程——所有人和工具应该如何协作。这些仍然是使用 agentic 系统的基础。你仍然需要目标，需要非常清楚想要什么结果。你必须了解不同模型的优势——过去是人，现在基本上是模型。不同的模型有不同的优势，就像它们有不同的"性格"，你得去了解它们、培养直觉，这样才能把正确的工具用在正确的场景上。
bilingual.en: The second lever is around okay what's the purpose? Does everyone know what they're supposed to do with their talents? Like do we have a goal? Do we have a purpose? And then the third thing is process which is how should all of these different people and tools come together. And these are still the fundamentals of working with agentic systems. Like you still need a goal. You need to be very clear about what the outcome is. And you have to understand the strengths of — you know used to be people but now it's basically models and different models have different strengths. So it's like they have different personalities and so you kind of have to get to know it like um you know develop an intuition for it so that you can use the right tools for the right purposes and I mean we talk about agents but we also talk about like what are the tools that agents have access to.

bilingual.zh: 所以你仍然需要做这些决策，然后还有流程——怎么做。随着模型越来越好，agent 会变得更聪明，能处理越来越高层次的问题。但我觉得我们仍然需要提供正确的上下文和高层指令，才能得到想要的结果。所以本质上，原则是一样的。我完全同意，我们越来越多人需要加倍投入这些技能，才能高效使用这些工具。
bilingual.en: So you still have to make decisions around that and then there's of course process which is how you do it. Now I think with better and better models, perhaps the agents get smarter so they can deal with higher and higher levels of figuring out how to do something, but I think it's still very important for us to be able to provide the right context, provide the right high-level instructions so that we get what we want. So really, it's the same principles. Uh and I absolutely agree with you that more and more of us are going to have to double down on these skills to be able to use these tools very effectively.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 沿着这个思路，我手上有你的书。你列了一个清单——管理者的工作是建立一个配合良好的团队、帮助成员实现职业目标、创建高效的工作流程。基本上就是你刚才说的。有趣的是，中间那条——帮助 agent 发展职业——你不用再操心了。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** So along those lines, I have your book right here. You have this list of a manager's job is to build a team that works well together, support members in reaching their career goals, and create processes to get work done smoothly and efficiently. Uh, which is basically exactly what you just said. Interestingly, that middle bullet is the part you don't have to worry about anymore with agents. You don't have to worry about their career development and progress. And

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 确实。不过有人开玩笑说，如果我们现在对 agent 不好，等 AGI 来了会怎样？也许对它们好一点还是值得的。我就是那种下 Waymo 会说谢谢的人，用 ChatGPT 语音模式时也会说"谢谢，这真的很有帮助"。沿着这个话题，在管理者的重要技能中，你觉得哪些在使用 agent 和 AI 系统时最有价值？我想到的是清晰度、沟通能力——你觉得应该加倍投入哪些技能？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** that's true. That's true. Uh, though some people do joke that if we don't treat our agents nice, what's going to happen when AGI comes? And, uh, you know, maybe it's still it still might benefit us to be kind. I'm one of those people that says thank you to the Waymo when I leave and just like thanks uh ChatGPT when I'm in voice mode. Just like thank you that was really helpful. So along these lines, I know there's a lot of ways to go here, but just in terms of skills that are important to a manager, which do you think are most valuable to develop in working with agents in AI systems? I think about things like clarity, communication, just like what comes to mind when you think about like here's the things you want to double down on as you're learning to be a manager that will also help you be really good at AI tooling and working with agents.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 首先是定义目标、定义结果，对成功的样子有极其清晰的认知。如果你让一家公司做这件事，我们都知道这对人类来说就很难。为什么大公司的对齐那么困难？往往就是因为不同的人对"成功是什么样"有不同的画面。即使我用人话描述"Lenny，我想做一个很棒的产品"或者"这期播客我想让很多人听到并有所收获"——这太笼统了。怎么才能具体到无歧义地判断是否达成？这真的是一个非常非常难的问题。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Like the first is defining the goal and defining the outcome and being really really crystal clear on what does success look like. I mean there's obviously lots and lots of — if you ask a company to do this we'll know that this is challenging for humans right I think a lot of times when you talk about why is alignment so difficult at a big company it often comes down to this question which is different people may have different pictures of what success looks like and even if I describe in human words oh you know Lenny I want to build this product and it's going to be amazing or this podcast episode — you asked me I want lots of people to hear it and take away things — that's very general. Like how do we get even more specific so that we know without question whether we've hit it or not. This is actually a really really difficult problem.

bilingual.zh: 对我们来说这是一个不同层面的难题，因为我们倾向于高层次思考。所以弄清楚如何把它细化到 agent 能真正理解什么是成功、什么是失败——这就是核心挑战。这也是为什么我们要写 eval，为什么 eval 如此重要——它帮我们理解客观标准是什么。现在我在做数据方面的工作，我的公司就是在尝试自动化数据分析，而永恒的问题是——数据、指标和 KPI 的意义就在于尽可能客观地衡量成功是什么样。我觉得这更像是一门艺术而不是科学。但如果你对成功是什么样都不清楚，你的 prompt 大概率不会得到最好的产出。这对管理团队是这样，对管理 AI 也一样。
bilingual.en: It's a different difficult question for us because again we tend to think very high levels. So figuring out how to boil it down so that an agent can really understand what success and failure looks like is a lot of the game. And I think this also relates to things like well that's why we have to write evals and that's why they're so important because they're helping us understand what is the objective criteria and these days I work in data and my company is all about trying to automate data analysis and the forever question — the whole point of data and the whole point of metrics and KPIs is we're trying to put a little bit more of an objective measure or get as crystal clear as possible about what success looks like and I think it's really an art more than it is like a science. But that's like the first thing I think if you're really unclear about what success looks like the prompt, you're probably not going to get the most amazing work. Um I think that's true for managing teams and it's very much true for managing AIs.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 好的，让我换个角度——我们来聊另一个趋势：组织扁平化。管理者被裁，所有人又变回 IC。我刚在播客上采访了 Airtable 的 CEO，他的核心观点是 CEO 必须重新成为 IC。他现在写代码比以前任何时候都多，他认为你必须深入细节才能知道产品该怎么做。我还看到一个数据说 Google 裁减了大量小团队的中层管理者。这就是一个扁平化的趋势。那么问题来了——未来我们还需要管理者吗？你怎么看？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Okay. So let me actually flip this on you and talk about another trend that we're seeing which is this kind of flattening of orgs. Uh managers being let go. Everyone's becoming an IC again. I just had the CEO of Airtable on the podcast and his whole shtick was that CEOs have to become ICs again. He's coding more than he's ever coded again and his feeling is you have to know what's possible by being in the weeds in order to figure out what your product should be. I also just saw a stat that Google let go of so many of their middle managers of smaller teams. It's just like this flattening trend. So, do we even need managers? I guess is one question in the future. And then just thoughts on how this uh will play out.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我觉得 AI 在工作场所带来的真正魔法是——每个个体变得强大得多。过去是这样的——我没有做十种不同工作的技能，所以我需要招人来做这些。我需要一个擅长设计的人，一个擅长编码的人，一个擅长数据分析的人，然后组建团队。但现在有了 AI 作为伙伴，等一下，AI 让我可以自己做很多这些工作了。我做不到顶尖 1% 或 10% 的水平，但如果我原来在零到第十百分位，AI 可以很快把我提升到第六十到七十百分位的水平。这打开了太多扇门。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah. So, I think the real promise and magic of AI that we're seeing in the workplace is that it leads us to each individual is far more empowered. So, it used to be okay, I don't have the skills to do 10 different jobs, so I need to supplement by hiring people to do these jobs. I need someone who's really good at design. I need someone who's really good at coding. I need someone who's really good at data analysis. And then I'll assemble that team. But now with AI and my companion, it's like, wait a second, AI makes — allows me to do many of those jobs myself. Now, I'm not going to do them at the — it's called the PhD or the highest 1% 10% level, but if I was, you know, at the zero or 10th percentile, it can certainly get me even today very quickly up to like the 60th 70th in terms of um what the state of the art is. And I think that that unlocks so many doors.

bilingual.zh: 所以我一直告诉团队的是——我们需要打破传统角色的边界。过去我们有传统团队配置——工程师、产品经理、设计师、研究员、数据科学家。但现在团队可以只有两个人，他们可以是任何传统学科背景的人，但关键是他们现在可以用 AI 来帮自己做很多过去需要其他人做的事情。所以某种意义上，我们可以抛掉所有这些角色区分，统一叫自己 builder。这是我觉得最通用的思考方式。我们都可以是 builder。我希望未来这就是所有人的头衔。
bilingual.en: And so the main thing that I felt so excited about and this is something I tell my team all the time is we need to dissolve the boundaries of these traditional roles. So in the past again we would have a traditional team — engineers, product manager, designer, researcher, data scientist — and I think now the teams can look more like well it's just two people and again they could be any of these traditional disciplines but uh the key thing is they can now use AI to help themselves do a lot of the things that the other folks used to be able to do. So, in some ways, we can drop all of these different role distinctions and call ourselves builders. I think that's sort of the most general purpose way of thinking about what we can all be. We can all be builders. And I'd love for us to get to a world where that's just — that's like the title. That's the term I've been actually using more and more.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我过去围绕"产品经理"来定位这个播客和通讯，后来开始用更宽泛的"产品"，现在我越来越多地用"builder"这个词。我喜欢这个词，因为正如你所说，各种角色的界限正在模糊。我很好奇，在你的公司里具体是什么样的？你们做了什么不同的事？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I used to orient this podcast as a newsletter around product managers and then I started using just product to be a little more broad and now I'm actually using that term builder. Uh so and I love that term because it's exactly what you're saying and this is very much a theme that comes up often in these conversations more and more just the lines are blurring. I'm curious at your company how does that look like? What are you doing differently? What are you seeing on the ground within your company that maybe would be different from a few years ago?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我们消除了更多角色。比如我们以为会需要一堆产品经理，但事实上如果你没有产品经理——我知道这可能有点违背
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So we have eliminated more roles. Um for example we thought we would need a bunch of product managers. It's turned out that actually um if you don't have a product manager, I know this might be going against a little bit of the ethos of of where

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 来了来了。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** here we go.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 违背 Lenny 的起家之道，但我发现当你有设计师或产品经理时，如果我是工程师，遇到"我需要搞清楚产品定义"这种问题，我的默认反应是"这是别人的工作职责，我把它委派出去"。但这样做其实错失了一个机会——作为工程师，我也应该深入思考该做什么、用户体验应该是什么样。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Where Lenny started, but I find that sometimes when you have like a designer or product manager and let's say I'm an engineer, then when I have a problem like, oh, I need to figure out the product definition, my default will be, well, I've got these people and that's kind of their job description, so I'm just going to delegate that to them. And I think that in doing so, you know, again, where we want to be polite, we want to respect everyone's lanes. I think that's a missed opportunity for that, you know, if I'm the engineer to look be like, wait a second, I should probably focus a lot too. Like I need to understand and have an opinion about what to build or what the user experience is.

bilingual.zh: 所以我们发现，如果把团队做得更小——即使在 AI 之前也是这样——减少这些角色，每个人都会意识到"我们没有产品经理了，沟通是我的事，弄清楚如何给用户创造最大价值现在完全是我的职责"。这就是为什么我非常支持把团队做小、消除这些分界线。当然，我不是说每个人都要做所有事。我们仍然尊重你在某个技能上比我强得多的事实，但这更多是关于具体场景，而不是角色。
bilingual.en: And so we found that if we actually make teams smaller and we kind of — even like in the past, you know, pre-AI — just have fewer of these, it allowed everyone to be like, oh wait, we don't have a product manager on the team. Okay, so communication's up to me. Figuring out how we get greatest value to users is something that is now strictly in my charter. And so that's why I'm such a big fan of like we can make teams smaller and we can eliminate these lines. Sure, again, there's still — I'm not trying to say like everyone has to do everything. We still can respect the fact that you might be much better at this particular skill than me, but it's less about the role and it's more about the specific context that we're in.

bilingual.zh: 我发现当你赋权团队根据具体场景采取行动，而不是用高层规则和流程来约束他们时，你会得到更好的工作、更快的速度，还有更快乐的员工——因为人们觉得自己真的有能力去创造想要的东西。
bilingual.en: And I find that whenever you have teams and you empower them to be able to take more action on their specific context rather than having these higher level rules or policies or like this is how it's supposed to be — then you get better work, you get faster work uh and you get happier employees because you know people feel like they actually can you know have the power to create the thing that they want.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这真的很有趣——仅仅是"没有 PM"这个约束就让工程师意识到不能等别人来做，必须自己搞定。但显而易见的挑战是他们得擅长这些。产品定义、优先级排序、获取对齐——这跟工程是非常不同的工作。你在招聘时会做什么不同的事，知道你可能不会招 PM？感觉很难找到各方面都很强的人。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** That's really interesting that just that constraint of not having a PM makes the engineer realize they're not going to wait for someone else to do it. They have to figure it out. The obvious kind of trick there is they have to be good at this. It's a very different job from engineering to be really good at articulating here's the problem we're going to solve. Here's why it's important that we're solving. Here's how we're going to prioritize everything we're thinking about. Here's how do we get alignment? Is there something you do differently when you're hiring these engineers knowing you're gonna probably not hire a PM — and that feels really hard to hire for someone that's really good at all these things?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 确实。我再说一次，我不是在说每个人都需要擅长所有事情，那不太现实。比如，如果要组建一个团队，几个工程师中没人擅长产品需求思考或用户视角，那我们可能确实需要补充一个有这种技能的人——可以是设计师、另一个擅长这个的工程师、传统 PM，甚至是一个很强的数据分析师。这种技能仍然重要，团队仍然需要具备，否则可能不会产出最好的结果。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** It is true. And I'm not trying to say again that everyone needs to be good at everything. I don't think that's very realistic. I do think for example, if we were going to create a team and we're going to have uh a couple engineers and none of them are very good at thinking through product requirements or what the user angle is, we probably do need to supplement the team with somebody with that skill set, right? And that might be a designer or that might be another engineer who's really good at that or that might be uh you know a traditional product manager or sometimes even a data analyst who's really good. So that skill is still important and the team still needs to have that skill otherwise it's probably not going to produce the best outcome.

bilingual.zh: 但我喜欢从"这个项目需要什么技能，我们能不能找几个人覆盖"来思考，而不是自动按照"需要一个 PM、一个设计师、三个工程师"的模板来。另一个例子是前后端工程师的区分。过去是"有些人是前端，有些是后端，项目有前端也有后端，那就各来一个"。但如果你说"你是工程师，你是 builder，这有一点前端工作，你大概能搞定——用 AI 帮你，让专家 review 代码就行，去做吧"。
bilingual.en: But I like to think of it as like what are the skills that are needed for this and can we now find a couple people — but it doesn't mean we just automatically go to that script of need a PM, need a designer, need three engineers, need that — you know, another example for us is like even thinking about frontend backend engineering. And it used to be like oh some people are front end engineers and some backend so if you have a project and it's got some front end some backend the shortcut is like I need one of these and one of these and that's how it's going to go. But if you say look you're an engineer, you're a builder. This has a little bit of front end but you know what you can probably figure that out. Like use AI to help you figure it out. You know get obviously someone who's a specialist to review the code or to give you some high-level guidance on things but just do it.

bilingual.zh: 自从我们开始这样做，初期确实需要一些投入——大家不太舒适，需要学习，所以一开始会慢一点。但长期回报巨大，因为现在有更多人更全面，能独立承担更多事情。当然，遇到特别重前端的项目，还是会请专家来。
bilingual.en: And ever since we started to implement that as well we see — yeah again a little bit of — you have to kind of invest a little bit in the beginning so people are not as comfortable they have to learn so you initially things take longer. It takes a little bit of extra time, right? Versus if you did slot in a front-end specialist and this is a front-end project, it probably would have gone a little bit shorter. But in the long run, that investment really pays off because now you have a lot more people who are again a little more well-rounded and can take on many more pieces just on their own. And then in specific scenarios like, oh, this is super front-end heavy. Sure, let's still bring in somebody who is more specialized in that particular skill.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我很喜欢你同时有在 Meta 这样巨型公司工作的经验，现在又在做自己的小创业公司，正好处在"保持精简、每个人做更多事"这个趋势中间。太酷了。几个问题——哪些职能被 AI 工具加速最多？是工程吗？有什么工具对你们特别有帮助？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I love that you've had the experience of working at a mega large company at Meta and now you're building your own startup that's small and in the middle of this trend of just staying very small and staying really lean and just everyone doing more things. It's so cool that you're experiencing that. So, a couple questions there. Just which functions are you seeing most accelerated with all these AI tools? Is it engineering? Is it something else? And then are there tools that have been most helpful to you? Just AI tools for folks who would be like, "Oh, I should check it out." Like I'm guessing cursor, but curious if there's anything else.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 工程肯定是。我们公司大部分是工程师，所以这是我们最关注的。但我也看到更多人在做原型。我们有大约两名设计师，但工程师也在做。我们有一个叫"product science"的团队——你可以理解为前线部署的人员，有数据分析背景，同时扮演客户成功和产品的角色。你能看到他们开始做更多原型，甚至涉足一些工程工作。看到每个人都能做一点其他领域的事，大家互相鼓励，这真的很棒。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah, certainly engineering. Engineering is one that we — I mean most of our company is engineers. So that's the one that we focused on a bunch. I certainly do see more people also prototyping things. And so it's not just — we have like two designers, but we also see engineers. Um we have one — we have a team that's called product science which is this interesting blend of — you can think about it as like a forward deployed person who is uh has a lot of data analysis background um and is kind of playing a customer success role and also kind of playing like a product role um and you see them starting to take on building more prototypes or getting into some of the engineering. And so it's really lovely to see that blend of everyone can do a little bit of everything else and we're all encouraging each other.

bilingual.zh: 我们最近还在做的一件事是——当我们说"工程师现在可以做数据分析"的时候，他们的第一反应是"我不太会分析"。这时候 ChatGPT 就派上用场了。传统上你会说"我得找人学，要占别人很多时间让他们给我解释"。但实际上我觉得现在 ChatGPT 和其他 AI 工具是更好的老师。
bilingual.en: Um the other thing that recently we've also been trying to do a lot more is just — you know obviously we say hey engineer now you can do analysis — and their first thing is like oh I don't really know analysis. This is where ChatGPT comes in and it's like well you know traditionally we would say well I have to learn that from a human. I have to ask this person and now I'm going to take a bunch of their time because I want them to explain everything to me. And in fact, I think these days, you know, ChatGPT or these other AI tools are better teachers.

bilingual.zh: 我们可能还没有充分利用这些工具来加速学习。我有时候会找一个在线课程——比如一个 12 周的课程大纲——然后喂给 ChatGPT，让它根据我的学习偏好来定制课程。比如我需要大量例子，需要像给五岁小孩解释一样简单的类比。我们团队里其他人的学习方式不同——有人觉得例子没用。所以一个能为每个人个性化学习的工具，真的能帮我们更快地学会这些技能。是的，Cursor 很好，能自动补全、帮你写代码。但学习加速可能是我们所有人工具箱中最被低估的功能——每次我跟人聊到这个，大家都说"对啊我们可以这样做"，然后还是忘了。坐下来花 30 分钟或一小时，学习速度比以前快得多。
bilingual.en: Um, I find that we tend to maybe not use them quite as much, you know, just for the purposes of accelerating our education or even going through something like — sometimes what I'll do is I'll find a curriculum online and, you know, if you like take a course, it'll be like this whatever 12-week curriculum and I'll just feed it into ChatGPT and I'll say help me customize a program for me, you know, using the ways that I like to learn. Like I am a person who really needs examples. I need a lot of like explain like I'm five, give me an analogy. And I know some other people on my team, they're like I don't get like these examples don't make any sense. Like we're different types of learners. And so the idea of like a tool that personalizes learning for each of us really helps us I think accelerate and just learn these skills much faster than before. So yes, the tools are great. We can use cursor. It helps us. It autocompletes. It writes a bunch of things. But the acceleration of learning I think is another maybe underutilized tool in all of our arsenals just because I know whenever I talk to people we just we forget — like we don't think that like oh wait yes we could be doing that — and just sitting down and probably in 30 minutes or an hour learn so much faster than what we used to be able to do before.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这个观点很有趣。一方面有即时帮你做事的工具，但你也需要学会怎么做事。你的团队在学什么？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** That's such an interesting point. There's like these tools that are in the just in time helping you move faster and then there's — but you also need to learn how to do something. Like some foundational lessons. Uh what's an area that your team did that — like what did they work on learning?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我举个例子。今天早上我跟一个工程师聊天，他写了很多算法。我们公司的事情之一是自动化数据分析。所以我们需要理解最佳实践——比如"哪些功能是人们真正付费的"这类问题，我们得弄清楚该用什么分析方法。这个工程师跟我说："Julie，我觉得我理解 how——我知道算法，我知道我们怎么做根因分析。但我不太理解 why 和 when——在什么业务场景下这些会用到？"因为他是工程师，没做过 PM 或高管那种会问这类问题的工作。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So I'll give you an example. I was just talking to an engineer this morning and he's written a bunch of these algorithms. So one of the things our company does is we're trying to automate data analysis. So one of the things we have to do is obviously understand the best practices like if there's a type of question — hey what features are really the ones that people pay for — we need to kind of figure out what is the right analysis to do. And so the engineer was saying to me you know Julie I feel like I really understand the how. Like I know the algorithms. I know we do root cause analysis. Like how we do that. But what I don't really understand is why or when this would be most useful. Like in what context in a company would this come up? Because he's an engineer. He hasn't done that job of being like a PM or an executive that asks these types of questions.

bilingual.zh: 这就是一个完美的用例——传统上你可能要找人问，但这是一个更通用的问题，互联网上有大量资源，ChatGPT 可能会给你更好的答案，让你深入理解。我们还在学习另一种用法——把 ChatGPT 当作学习测试。它解释一堆东西后，我会尝试用自己的话复述回去。然后 ChatGPT 会评判我——"对，你说得对"或者"不太对"。它总是先礼貌地说"差不多了"，然后最终告诉你"你完全错了"——但就是用很客气的方式。这种互动特别有帮助，因为你可以通过用自己的方式重述来真正检验是否理解了概念。
bilingual.en: And that was like the perfect thing where like yeah you know traditionally you might have asked someone but this is more general purpose — like there's so much resources in the world on the internet about it — this is like the perfect type of question where if you just talk to ChatGPT it's probably going to give you a much better answer and allow you to go deeper. And a secondary thing we've been learning too is this idea of — almost like using ChatGPT — it's like you test your learning. So it explains a bunch of things and so what I often like to do is like okay I read this — so does this mean — try to explain back what I heard right. So does this mean is the right way to think about it that this is kind of like this analogy? And ChatGPT will critique me. Yes that is right or no you didn't quite get that right. Like in fact and it always tries to say it nicely — this is a funny part — like that's close and then eventually it's like you were completely wrong — just in the style — but like it helps so much because it's interactive and so we can really test whether we really understand the concept by trying to retell it back in our own way.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** AI 突破帮助我们进步的方式实在太多了——做更多事、学更多东西、变得更好。我知道有一些缺点，但这太不可思议了。我想多聊聊数据分析。你有一条很有趣的轨迹——从大公司出来创业，从设计主管变成痴迷数据。那些已经想明白如何用 AI 做数据分析的公司在做什么不同的事？大家在用数据方面有什么遗漏？感觉我们在一个个解锁团队的瓶颈——等 PM 写 PRD 是一个，等数据科学家给分析结果是另一个。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** It's incredible just how many ways all this AI breakthrough is helping us advance and do more and learn more and become better. I know there's some downsides but this is incredible and so many ways of getting better and faster. I want to spend a little more time on this data analysis stuff. So again, you have such an interesting trajectory from working at a big company to starting your own small company. From being head of design to now being obsessed with data and analytics. So let me spend a little time there. Um what do AI companies that have kind of figured out how to use AI for data analysis and data work doing differently? What can people — what are people missing and sleeping on in terms of getting better at working with data? And let me just add this point. It feels like we're almost working through here's all the blockers to a team moving forward. There's like a waiting for the PM to write the PRD and then there's like waiting for the data scientist to give you answers analysis. So this is like another really cool unblock that every team member will have.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 你第一个问题是 AI 公司怎么用数据。有意思的是——我其实不觉得很多快速增长的公司在这方面做得好。主要原因是传统上公司不会增长那么快。如果你做到一亿用户，公司可能已经存在很多年了。你有时间搭建 logging，招了增长团队和数据团队，做了大量仪器化工作——你通常有多年时间来做这些。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So your first question was how are a bunch of AI companies using data. So the funny thing, my funny answer to this is I don't actually think a lot of the fast growing companies are using data well at this point. And the main reason why is because traditionally things just didn't grow that fast. And so you know if you got to 100 million users, your company's probably been around for a while. And if your company's been around for a while, you've had time to set up things like logging and you've hired a growth team at that point and you've hired a data team and they've done a bunch of work to log and instrument and then transform the data and like we've talked about like what is the observability for our business and you just usually had years to build and develop that because of the rate of growth.

bilingual.zh: 但今天我们看到疯狂增长的公司，团队可能就十个人或两个人，却有数亿美元的 ARR 和数亿用户——但他们没有那些基础设施、logging 和数据工具来真正做数据分析。所以这些公司完全是靠好直觉和好 vibes 在跑。确实，有时候你不需要数据分析也能做出有用的东西。但数据能帮我们做的是反映真实情况。
bilingual.en: And so today we see companies that are growing insanely fast and they're still about 10 people or two people or however many people but they've got hundreds of millions in ARR and hundreds of millions of users and you know what they don't actually have all of that infrastructure — that logging and all — to be able to truly do data analysis. So I would say that these companies are totally getting by on just good instincts and good vibes. And we see that, right? Like you don't really need um a data analysis to sometimes make something that works. But I think what data helps us do is it sort of is like helping us reflect back what is really reality.

bilingual.zh: ARR 在增长？太好了，继续做你在做的事。但总有一天增长会停下来。增长不会永远持续。当增长停下来时，所有人都在问"怎么了为什么？"——这时候如果你对业务有很好的可观测性（observability），就更容易找到根因，甚至提前预测增长是否会放缓。如果你没有好的 observability，你就会手忙脚乱。那时候大家才开始疯狂投入数据建设。
bilingual.en: And so of course if ARR is growing, awesome, you know, great, keep doing what you're doing. But what always happens is eventually things stop growing. Growth does not happen forever. And usually when growth stops everyone has this question of like what's going on why did it happen and then you start to be able to see the power of — if you've instrumented everything very well and you have a very good observability model for your business — it's much easier to start to get into the root cause. It's easier to even predict whether growth will slow down at a certain point. It's easier to catch these trends earlier. If you don't have good observability over how your business runs and what the company's key levers are, then you will be scrambling. And at that point, that's usually when people start investing a ton in data.

bilingual.zh: 所以很多热门公司还没到那个阶段。但我也看到另一个趋势——每次出现新的技术变革，我们实际上需要改变分析方法论。分析要回答我们的问题，如果技术或场景变了，我们就需要新的方法论。比如移动互联网时代——看 session 数、日均 session、移动端时长、session 长度，这些都是我们理解"用户在新媒介中获得价值了吗"的重要指标。今天也一样。会话式分析完全不同。过去在 Google——你点了 Shopping tab 我就知道你对购物感兴趣，点了 Maps tab 就知道要导航，我们可以衡量点击。
bilingual.en: So, I wouldn't say that a lot of these hot companies are quite there yet. But what I also think is a trend is that every time there's a new technological shift, we actually have to change the way that we think about analysis — analysis has to answer the questions that we have. And if technology changes or context changes, we need new methodologies of analysis. So for example when mobile came to the forefront right — looking at sessions or sessions per day or time spent on mobile or length of sessions became something that was important for us to understand. Are people getting value in this new medium? I think that's the same with what we have today. Conversational analytics is totally different. Used to be — let's say in the Google world right — I knew you were interested in shopping if you clicked the shopping tab. I know you're interested in maps if you click the maps tab. We can measure clicks.

bilingual.zh: 今天一切都是对话。要分辨用户意图变得更难了。如果我在做 LLM 产品，最大的问题之一是——哪些用例在增长？哪些在萎缩？这比过去难得多，因为不再是点击和页面的事了。你可能得用 LLM 或机器学习模型来对用户意图进行分类。你得问"对话进展顺利吗？如果用户只问了一个问题就没有来回，用户获得价值了吗？"我们始终在试图弄清楚这是不是一个好体验，但现在需要发明新的方法论来帮助分析。
bilingual.en: Today, it's just all conversation. And so, it's actually harder for us to tease apart what is the user intent. You know, if I worked on um any of these LLM products, I would say like one of the probably the biggest questions is, hey, what use cases are growing or what use cases are shrinking? And that's much harder to tell today because it's not just clicks on tabs or pages. It's like we have to probably use an LLM or a machine learning model to bucket user intent. We probably have to ask questions like is the flow going really well in conversations — you know like if I just ask one question and I don't go back and forth — like did the user get value, right? It's always trying to get back to like we're trying to figure out if this was a good experience, but now we need to actually invent new methodologies to help us analyze that.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 对，对话到底是长的好还是短的好？什么才是正确答案？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yeah, I think the question is always uh like with conversations, do you want it to be a long conversation? Do you want to be a short conversation? Like what's the right answer? What's better?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我之前采访了 ChatGPT 的负责人 Nick Turley，结果发现他们早期找到最常见用例的方式之一是看 TikTok 上的评论和病毒视频。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I had uh the head of ChatGPT on the podcast, Nick Turley, and turns out one of the ways they found the most common use cases early on was watching uh TikTok comments and things going viral on TikTok after they launched.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 关于那个。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** About that.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yep.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 好的。我想回到你这个很有趣的不寻常路径——从 Facebook 设计主管做起，你是那么多设计师的偶像，现在却把时间花在一个数据创业公司、痴迷数据。通常设计师不太喜欢实验和数据驱动决策。你觉得设计师在抗拒数据时，他们错过了什么？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Okay. So I want to come back to this really interesting uh unusual path that you took from uh being a head of design at Facebook. Uh you're an inspiration to so many designers. Now you spend your time on a data startup obsessed with data. Uh I don't know — classically designers aren't the biggest fans of experiments and data and making decisions based on data. When you look at designers and you hear designers kind of push back on like no we don't want to be super data driven. And we want to uh we know better — we have a sense of what's beautiful and great and intuition all these things. What do you think designers are missing when they feel that and say that — when they're afraid of running experiments and data and kind of want to push that out?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 有一句话是我和联合创始人很早就反复讨论的，也分享给了很多合作的公司——你真正想要的是"用数据诊断，用设计治疗"。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** There's one phrase that my co-founder and I would always discuss amongst ourselves very early on and which we shared with a lot of the companies that we work with — which is what you really want is you want to diagnose with data and treat with design.

bilingual.zh: 数据不是一个告诉你该做什么的工具，也不会告你解决方案是什么，更不会帮你治好留存不好的问题。它做不到。但它能告诉你有没有问题、问题或机会在哪里。你仍然需要回去经历一个创造性过程来找到最好的解决方案。所以数据帮你弄清楚真正在发生什么——人们喜欢什么？在使用什么？不用什么？它只是给你一个更接近现实的故事。因为我们都有自己想相信的故事——"我的公司太棒了，人们爱我们"——但现实可能是另一幅图景。数据在做的就是捕捉现实。
bilingual.en: So data is not a tool that's going to tell you what you should build or what the solution is or how we're going to, you know, cure the fact that you don't have really great retention. It's just not. But it can tell you if you have a problem and where that problem or opportunity might be. But you still need to go back and undergo a very creative process to figure out what's the best way to solve that. So that's the first thing I would say — this framework of like data helps you figure out what's actually happening. What do people like? What are they engaging with? What not? Right? It just gives you a story that better reflects reality. Um because again, we all have stories, right? We're like, "Oh, my company's amazing. People love us." Blah blah. That's the story I want to believe. But reality may be a different picture. And so what data is trying to do is capture reality.

bilingual.zh: 顺便说一下，我眼中的数据不只是 A/B 测试和可量化的东西。人们在 TikTok 上发了什么、什么东西火了、他们在 Twitter 或者现在叫 X 上说什么——如果你做用户访谈，那些也都是数据。只是更难提炼和量化，虽然现在有了 AI 我们有更好的综合工具。所以在我看来这些都是数据——都在帮助我们理解现实中到底在发生什么。你仍然需要去发明、创造、做梦。没有公式、没有科学能精确告诉你怎么做出爆款。你可以做实验，可以更严谨地理解短期效果。但测试不能告诉你长期会怎样。你仍然需要综合判断。所以我说——用数据诊断，用设计治疗。
bilingual.en: And by the way, I don't think of data just as like it's AB test and it's quantitative things we can measure. To me data is also well what did people put onto TikTok and which things went viral and what are they saying in the Twitter-verse or X-verse I guess is what it's called now — and um and what are you know and if you do a customer interview like what — like that's still all data. It's just that that is you know a little harder to distill and quantify although now with AI you know we have better tools for synthesizing. So that's all data in my mind and it's just all trying to help us understand what is really happening, what is the phenomenon that's happening in reality and how do we understand it. You still have to go and invent and create and dream and there's no formula and there's no science that will tell you exactly how you're going to make a hit. Um you can experiment which allows you to try more things maybe and more rigorously understand what that does in the short term. You're going to have to — it's all very contextualized right because tests don't tell you what will happen in the very long run. And that's still data — you still have to synthesize and figure out what to do. So that's this thing I'll say — diagnose with data and treat with design.

bilingual.zh: 第二点，我觉得有时候我们会陷入数字的"虚假精确感"。数字涨了，我们就觉得太好了。但你选择看什么指标本身就是一门艺术。数字涨了 5%，这算好吗？这也是一种解读，也是艺术。有时候我们想要掌控一切，想要确定性——"如果做了 ABC，一切都会很好"。设计师在这一点上的反击往往是对的——"现实就是模糊的，有不确定性，我们永远无法确知。"
bilingual.en: I think the second thing I will usually tell designers about is I find that sometimes — maybe it's the let's call it the false precision of numbers that we kind of fall into, right? Because it's like okay we got these numbers and the numbers go up. It's like no, the fact that you still have to choose which things you look at is an art, not a science. And your interpretation of if the number went up 5%, is that good? Is that not good? Is also an interpretation and is an art, not a science. It's just that sometimes I think we can give ourselves this feeling — and I get it — like we sometimes there's this instinct to want to control things and we want everything to be buttoned up and we want to know that if we did ABC everything will be great. Our career is going to be awesome. Our product's going to rocket ship. And I think designers are rightly often pushing back and saying, "No, the reality is like this stuff is ambiguous and there's uncertainty and we can never know for sure." And I think all that is quite true.

bilingual.zh: 我还想说的是——你绝对不可能靠 A/B test 做出一个伟大的产品。我坚信这一点。但我不觉得我们应该因噎废食。不是数据或设计，而是两者都是我们可以使用的工具。我见过的每一个出色的设计师都痴迷于更好地理解现实。他们想知道用户真正想什么、真正在做什么。如果能读每个用户的心思，那就是我们所有设计师梦寐以求的事。数据正在尝试帮我们做到这一点。它不完美，但不意味着我们不能用它来提升产品开发。
bilingual.en: So the other thing I would say that I really support is like you just actually can't make a really great product by thinking you can AB test your way into it. So that's — I fundamentally believe that. But I don't think we should throw the baby out with the bath water. I think there's actually — you know — these it's not either or. It's not like data or design. It's like these are just tools for us to use. And I would say every amazing designer that I've ever met is absolutely obsessed with trying to get a better understanding of reality. They want to know what users really think. They want to know what they're really doing. If they could read every user's minds, that's like the thing we would all want, right? As a designer is like, if I could just know what everyone is thinking and feeling every time they used it, my life would be a lot easier because then I would be able to build better and better things. And so that's what it's trying to help us do. It isn't perfect. No metric is going to tell you whatever we hope that it can in terms of the true certainty and precision, but it doesn't mean we can't use it to better our product development.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我正想说你说的这些。我合作过的每一个优秀设计师都是最痴迷数据的那个——反而是不够好的设计师在说"我有直觉，何必看数据"。正如你说的，数据不会告诉你该做什么，但会告诉你机会在哪里。让我们回到管理话题。你觉得 AI 崛起之后，管理者的角色和日常工作最大的变化是什么？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I was going to say exactly what you just said, which is every great designer that I've worked with was obsessed with data — in the most leaning into the data — versus designers that are just like, nah, I think I'm going to — I have a sense of what's right and why would we let that tell us what to do? And to your point, it's not going to tell you what to do. It'll tell you where opportunities arise. Let me take us back to the management chat and maybe just let me ask something broad. What do you feel is the biggest change in the role and day-to-day work and life of a manager these days with the rise of AI?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 管理变化一直是管理者的工作，总有各种混乱在发生。但我觉得变化的速度在加速。过去几十年我们已经看到了。现在人们面临更大的不确定性——两年后 AI 会在什么位置？谁真的知道？5 年后我们会有 AGI 吗？这会极大改变整个格局。更不用说很多人对职业感到恐惧——"如果我的职业一直是设计，而 AI 越来越擅长我做的事，那我的职业和未来怎么办？我需要转型吗？"
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I think that managing change — it's always been manager's job to manage change and there's always the chaos of what's going on. I just think the rate of change is accelerating and we've seen that over the last couple of decades. And so I find that there's just a great deal more uncertainty that people have about things like — we all — you know where is AI going to be in two years from now? I don't know. Who really knows, right? And so you know are we going to have AGI in 5 years? That kind of changes a lot about the landscape. Not to mention I think there's quite a lot of fear that many organizations are feeling. You know it's like if my career has always been in design and now these tools are getting better and better at what I'm doing, then holy — what happens to my career and my future and do I need to pivot? Do I need to learn different things? And so, it's this change. It's this feeling of uncertainty.

bilingual.zh: 管理者不仅要处理这些情绪，还要学习新技能——管理 AI 和更强大的工具。这跟十年、二十年、三十年前非常不同。所以更重要的技能是沟通、反馈、同理心——和人类协作，让他们理解我们正在经历变化。每个领导者现在都要做这件事——怎么传达"事情在变，我们必须对变化保持开放态度。如果固守旧方式，我们会被甩在后面。"
bilingual.en: And I think a lot of times managers have to deal with that in addition to what you were saying before, which is they also have to learn these new skills, which is managing AI and managing kind of like these more powerful tools in their arsenal of trying to get things done. So that is very different I think than maybe you know 10, 20, 30 years ago. Um and so I think that the skills that become more important are obviously communication, feedback, compassion, but just being able to work with humans and to have them understand that like yes, we are in a state of change. I think every leader has to do this now. Every startup founder that I know, every CEO is — how do you land this message that things are changing and we need to be very open to change. If we go and stick to our old ways, we're probably going to get left behind. Uh our product's going to get left behind. Even our way of doing things is going to be left behind. So, we need to change, right? We need to change our product and we need to change the way that we work as we all talked about in terms of smaller teams, more nimble, blah blah blah.

bilingual.zh: 但同时——怎么做到不让所有人恐慌？所以我经常想到柳树的隐喻——柳树是一棵非常坚固的树，能经受住风暴和灾难。但它的枝条非常柔软——而正是这种柔软让它坚固。所以今天的管理本质上就是"坚固的同时保持灵活"。这很难做到，但我总是告诉自己——像柳树一样，想象柳树，把那种感觉带入你和团队正在做的事中。
bilingual.en: Um but at the same time it's like how do we do that in a way that doesn't just freak everyone out and it's like ah it's chaos everything's changing like — so I think about this metaphor a lot of like the willow tree which is like the willow tree is a very sturdy tree. You know it's it can survive a lot of storms, disasters, etc. But it's also very flexible. Like the branches are very very flexible and that's in some ways what allows it to be very sturdy. So I think today management is really about this idea of like be sturdy while being flexible and that is a very hard thing to pull off but I think that's like — at least when I even go into it — like be like the willow tree Julie just imagine the willow tree and like try and channel that as the kind of feeling of of what it is that we're trying to do together.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这让我想到其他嘉宾说过的话。Mark Benioff 上我的播客时，我问他怎么应对这些变化。他说他永远都是"太好了，这正是我们想要的，太令人兴奋了，有这么多机会，一点都不无聊，我们总能重新发明"。他总是在拥抱变化。我永远忘不了他的回答。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** This reminds me of a couple things from other guests. Uh I had Mark Benioff on the podcast and I asked him just how do you deal with all this change? Things are — it's like agents now. It was uh I don't know — AGI coming as you said — just like how do you survive through this? And his advice is just he's like I'm always just like good this is great I this is what we want this is exciting we have so much opportunity it's just not boring we can always reinvent and he's always embracing it. And I just I'll never forget the way he responded to that.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 如果你不觉得变化是好事，接下来的日子会过得很痛苦。所以如果条件相同，就倾向于拥抱它。如果你每天醒来能把它看作机会和兴奋而非恐惧——虽然这只是同一枚硬币的两面。但如果管理者假装一切都好、没人不开心，那也缺失了什么。要能说出来："是的，变化很难。我们可能会沮丧，会经历混乱。但我们会挺过去，因为我们会保持灵活，我们会把目光放在令人兴奋的可能性上。"
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I think if you don't think it's good it's kind of a painful way to live. It'll be very very difficult over these next — So, I do think that all things being equal, lean into it. Like, if you can wake up every day and see it as opportunity and excitement rather than fear. Again, they're all flip sides of the same coin. But I think if we can lean more into what could it be while recognizing that the other side does exist and it's still there. And I think if managers who try to pretend like it isn't there, like it's all good, no one's upset, etc. — but there's something also missing about just addressing and being able to be like, "Yeah, it's hard. Change is hard. We're probably going to get upset. We're going to have some chaos. Like, this is going to happen, but we will work through it because we're going to be flexible and we're going to be able to put our eyes on the big picture of what is possible, which is exciting."

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 还有一句话我印象很深，可能是 Kevin Weil 或 Mike Krieger 说的——"现在是有史以来最正常的时候。"
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** There's another quote that stuck — that kind of came up as you were talking. I forget who it was exactly. Maybe Kevin Weil, maybe Mike Krieger. They said that this is the most normal things will be ever.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 只会越来越奇怪。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Like it will only get weirder.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 给人们一种感觉——珍惜现在的"正常"吧，因为只会越来越奇怪——至少给人们真实的预期。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** And I think giving people that sense of like, okay, just enjoy this normal because this is going to be only weirder — because we'll at least give people an expectation, real expectations of where things might be going.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的，是的。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yes. Yes.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 活在当下真好。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** What a time to be alive.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 确实。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** What a time.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 好，我想把视角拉远一点。抛开 AI 不谈，管理在很多方面没有变——仍然是管人、帮助他们成功、产出好的工作。你觉得哪些最经典、最重要的管理经验，尤其是新管理者还没有完全理解的？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Okay. Let me uh zoom out even further and chat about — I want to ask you just outside of AI — management in many ways is unchanged. It's still a lot of the same work managing people, helping them be successful, producing great work. What are just some of the — I'd say maybe most timeless, most important uh lessons that you think managers, especially new managers, still don't totally understand, need to hear more. What are just some that come to mind and then we'll see where this goes.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 第一个想到的是管理自己、理解自己的重要性。这是我书里的第五章，叫"管理自己"。其实写的时候我想把它放在第一章，但出版商说也许应该先讲一些战术性的东西——人们不一定会认为管理他人和团队要从自己开始。但我真的相信这一点。我们每个人都有擅长的和不擅长的。我坚信每个优势本身就是一个劣势，每个劣势也是一个优势。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** The first thing that comes to mind is the importance of managing yourself and understanding yourself. This was chapter five of my book. It's called managing yourself. In fact, when I wrote it, I kind of wanted it to be chapter one. And then my publisher was like, well, maybe you should get into some of the tactical — like people don't necessarily think managing other people or managing a team starts with them. But I really do fundamentally believe this because I think all of us — of course like any human being — we have things that we're strong at. We have things that we're weak at. And I am a very big believer that every strength is its own weakness and every weakness is a strength.

bilingual.zh: 你不可能把每个维度都做到 100%。我觉得一个最有趣的概念是"维度性"。意思是你是一个人，但我们可以从无限个维度来看你。比如 Lenny 扔斧头的水平怎么样？这是一个维度。
bilingual.en: There's no such thing as you're going to somehow, you know, get every dimension to be 100%. In fact, I think one of the most interesting concepts — or frameworks for myself and also even kind of like a data frame concept — is this concept of dimensionality. So what dimensionality means is like you're a human being but we can kind of look at you in infinite dimensions. There is for example how good is Lenny at throwing an axe? That's one dimension.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 还挺好的。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Pretty good.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** Lenny 主持播客的水平怎么样？太棒了。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** How good is Lenny at uh being a podcast moderator? Fantastic.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 好吧，谢谢。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** So — okay, thank you.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** Lenny 在 AI 领域做从零到一项目的能力怎么样？你看，你可以想象无限个维度，每个人的剖面都像指纹一样独特。有些领域你在前 1%，有些在前 10%，有些是平均水平，有些低于平均。所有人都是这样。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** How good is Lenny at doing a zero to one type of project in the AI space? Right. So again just you can think about these as infinite dimensions and the reality is each of our profiles is very unique. It's like a fingerprint you know. So for you it's like these are all these areas that you're really great at — you know much better — like in the top 1% — and then there's some areas we're in the top 10% then there's some areas where you're like kind of average and then there's some dimensions in which you're worse than average compared to other people. And that's just true for all of us.

bilingual.zh: 我喜欢这个框架的原因是——如果你接受这个模型，你会意识到没有哪个维度等于你整个人。我可以说"Lenny，你的扔斧头水平真的需要提高"，理想情况下你不会觉得"Julie 在说我是个差劲的人，我的身份认同受到威胁"——因为这只是你无限维度中的一个。但有时候我们会过度依附于某些维度，开始觉得那就是自己。这时候就很难进行客观对话——"你在哪方面可以变得更好？"
bilingual.en: And what I like about that is therefore if you take that as the model, right? Things then — you realize that none of these dimensions are you entirely. So we can be — you know I can make a comment like Lenny your ax throwing really could use some improvement. And ideally you're not like Julie is saying I'm a bad person. I am a — my identity is at risk. Right? Because it's just one dimension of who you are. Uh but what happens sometimes is that we can get very attached to certain dimensions because we start to think that that's who we are and I think managers can do that. Um and uh clearly individuals, you know, on their teams. And when that happens, it starts to get very difficult to have, I think, more objective conversations about, okay, what can you get better at? What can you get worse at?

bilingual.zh: 我说这些是因为这个框架帮我和很多人意识到——别人给你反馈，或者你在某些维度不太好，都不代表那就是你。你是所有这些无限维度的总和。没有哪个维度代表你的真正价值。我坚信每个人都是美丽而有价值的——我们有各种技能，当然我们想提升，但这不代表我们是否有价值。
bilingual.en: And so, I say all this because I think this framework for me at least and many people that I've talked to has helped them realize that somebody can give you feedback or you can be maybe not great at certain dimensions. You can have room to improve and that's not who you are because you are all of these infinite dimensions in one. And none of them is representative of like your true worth as an individual. I am a big believer that we are all beautiful and worthy and sure we have all of these skills and yes we want to improve those skills but it does not speak to like whether we are worthy or not by saying whether we are strong or weak in these skills.

bilingual.zh: 如果你能真正内化这一点，你就能更客观地看待自己作为管理者的表现。我举个例子——我过去在绩效评估中经常被告知："Julie，你非常有深度、很 thoughtful，你思考问题的方式显然是经过深思熟虑的。"这是好的一面。但同时我也会收到反馈："Julie，你在讨论中不太说话，你比较安静，反应不够快。"
bilingual.en: And so I think if you can take that and really internalize that then you can look at yourself a little bit more objectively as a manager and you can realize that there are areas where you're going to be really strong. There are areas where you have biases and often they are one and the same. So I'll give an example. People have often told me — like I would get this in my performance reviews from managers in the past — like hey Julie you're really thoughtful. Uh so you know when you think about something you have like a way to think about it. You've clearly thought about it in depth and you've got like these frameworks and all that. That's a great thing. And then on the flip side I'll get feedback like well Julie you know you don't really say a lot um in a dynamic discussion. Like you're kind of quiet and uh you know you don't really think that quickly on your feet.

bilingual.zh: 你会发现这两者其实是同一件事的两面。正因为我不是那种脱口而出的人，我才能做到深思熟虑。至少在年轻时，这很明显——那个特定的"劣势"正是某个特定"优势"的体现。我就是那种不总有即时判断的人，我需要真正想清楚、内化、搞清自己怎么想，然后才能分享给大家。了解自己的这一点非常重要。
bilingual.en: And what you realize is like these are kind of — again — two things.
