Because I don't do that, and I'm not just off the cuff, that's what allows me to oftentimes be very very thoughtful, right? Or like that at least — again when I was younger — it's sort of it's very clear that that particular weakness is also very much speaking to a particular strength, which is I am the kind of person that doesn't always have a snap judgment. I have to really think about it and internalize it and sometimes get to how I feel and then I can share it and present it in the world. And so just knowing that about me is supremely helpful.

bilingual.zh: 当然这不意味着我永远不能变好。但我觉得"掌握"就是意识到这两面我们都可以提升——关键是在具体场景中判断该偏向哪一边。我拿到反馈后会想：好的，我需要学习怎么在面对面时更开放，更清楚地表达想法。比如说"我还不确定怎么想，但目前我在想的是这个"。这是明确的战术，可以让我在团队中更有效。
bilingual.en: Um now doesn't mean of course that I can never get better at this thing. But what I often think about is mastery is where we realize that both of these we can get better at. And what we want to do is just figure out in the context what makes sense to be. So I got this feedback and I'm like cool. One of the things I need to work on is figuring out how to be more open in person, how to speak a little bit more clearly in person. Maybe say things like, "I don't know exactly how I feel about it yet, but this is what I'm thinking right now." Like, there's still clear tactics that will allow me to be a more effective team member and to do a better job in the context of what I'm trying to do with my team.

bilingual.zh: 我在培养这些技能，但元技能是能退后一步说——在某些场景下快速果断非常重要，不完美也要行动。如果我在这方面有困难，我应该认识到这是需要改进的地方。但在另一些场景下，正确的做法是退后深思，不要仓促决定。所以我不是要拒绝某个优势或劣势，而是知道自己的起点在哪里。成长往往意味着学会做相反的事，但不是否定自己擅长的东西，而是随着时间找到平衡——能够读懂场景，知道该更深思熟虑还是更果断。
bilingual.en: So I've tried to build those skills but the meta skill is now being able to step back and say okay in certain contexts it is really important that we move fast and we are decisive and we just do something and even if it's not perfect we just kind of have to do it and if I struggle with that I should realize that that's an area to improve upon. But there are other contexts in which the right thing to do is actually to take a step back and be very thoughtful and to not rush into decisions. And so that's like — so what I want to get to is not like let's reject this strength or this weakness, but just know that that's like where we come from. That's naturally we might be wired in a particular way. Our growth often looks like getting better at doing the opposite, but not rejecting again the thing that we're good at, but rather over time getting to this balance where we can read the context in the situation and know should I lean a little more thoughtful or is this a time where I need to try and be a little more decisive and just share what's on my mind right now?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我很喜欢这个建议——我们最强的地方也有缺点，而我们得到的反馈往往指向我们不擅长的地方，但那恰好是人们欣赏的另一面的代价。我本来想问你——你收到"Julie 你在会议上说得不够多"的反馈时怎么办？一种选择是"这就是我，我用其他方式解决问题"。但我听到你说的是——在特定场景下主动改变那个行为，即使不是自然而然的。你建议人们在多大程度上去弥补不擅长的地方，versus 进一步发挥优势？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I love this advice that things that we are incredible at have a downside and oftentimes the feedback we're getting is something we're not great at. There's like a good version of that that people appreciate. And I was going to ask you — and I think you answered most of this — but just when you got this feedback of, hey, Julie, you're not speaking enough in these meetings. You're not uh contributing quickly enough. Uh it sounds like so one option is just like, okay, cool. That's me. That's how I am. And I'm just going to solve the problem this other way and I'm just not going to change anything. What I heard you say is find opportunities where that's actually — you want to actually change that behavior even though it doesn't come naturally — in specific situations where things are moving fast. Um I guess just how far do you recommend people push themselves in things they're not great at versus leaning further into their strengths?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 这个问题很好。我的思考方式是——完全取决于你的目标。比如 IC 和管理者的路径。IC 通常是想深化一门手艺——你热爱它，想在这个特定技能上越来越好。在我们的无限维度中，你选几个说"我要做到前 0.1%"。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I think that's a really great question. So the way I think about it is it is very dependent on what is your goal. So for example, let's say that you are — let's even take for example ICs versus managers. I think often about the pathway of an IC, an individual contributor, as wanting to deepen a craft. Like you just you love this thing and you just want to get better and better and better at this very specific skill or this craft, right? So think about in our dimension infinite — like you pick a couple dimensions like I just want these to be I want to be like the top 0.1% and that's kind of the pathway of extending as an IC.

bilingual.zh: 如果你的目标是"我想每天花十个小时做这件事，因为我热爱它，我想靠它养活自己"——那你就看看只深化这些维度，有没有一条路能达到你的目标。如果有，当有人问"你想当管理者吗？"你可以说"不用了"。
bilingual.en: Now, if that's your high level goal and you're like, I want to be able — let's say your high level goal is, I want to be able to do this, you know, 10 hours a day because I love it and I want to be able to support myself doing it, meaning I get paid and I have like a great job and I want to have a bunch of impact in the world by doing this thing, right? So, again, you still have goals. Then you have to see okay does my strategy of just deepening these things — is there a pathway to reach my goals according to that? And if there is awesome then if someone's like hey do you want to be a manager you're like nope don't need to because these are my goals and this pathway actually allows me to do that.

bilingual.zh: 但如果某天你发现你真正想完善的技能在商业上不太行得通——不能让你买到想买的大房子来养家——那你得问自己，光做这个不够，你可能需要学习一些其他技能。所以关键是回到你的目标。有时候深化手艺能支撑目标，有时候不能。痛苦往往是因为这两者不对齐——你想要大房子，但你只想花所有时间做完美的鸡蛋卷。你就陷在这种张力中，很难满足——"为什么世界不认可我深厚的鸡蛋卷技能？"好吧，你可以做鸡蛋卷大师，但可能得放弃那个目标——或者如果你想要那个目标，也许需要扩展你的技能范围，开一家米其林餐厅之类的。
bilingual.en: But if somehow you get to a point where — I don't know — the skill you really want to perfect is not something that may be commercially viable in the world — that's going to somehow allow you to buy the big mansion that you want to buy to support your family — then I think you have to ask yourself, okay, so if I just do this, it's not going to cut it. I might actually need to learn some of these other skills in order to be able to fulfill the job that is going to be valuable enough that people are going to pay me a bunch of money at this certain level so that I can afford my mansion.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 非常好的建议。不是简单地"一定要补短板"或"别管弱点"。而是——如果你需要这个技能来实现目标，那就去练。比如如果你想当 VP，你可能需要在重要会议上表现出色，不能等会后发邮件分享想法。
bilingual.en: So, I just think it has to go back to like what are your goals? And there are cases in which yes it will support your goal to do this and to deepen your craft and there are cases in which it won't and I think it's important — it's a very individual question for each person — but what I often think suffering is, is when these things are not aligned. So what you want is like the giant mansion and all of that, but you're like but I also just want to spend all my time perfecting my egg omelette. And then you're just like in this tension place and it's very hard to feel satisfied and fulfilled because you're a little bit like oh why doesn't the world value my deep egg omelette skills. Um like okay you can be the egg omelette person — you just have to you know maybe not do this thing — or if you want this thing you may actually need to be better at just egg omelettes — like perhaps you need to expand your repertoire of cuisines and like go and build a Michelin star restaurant or something.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 没错。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** This is really good advice. It's not just like definitely always work on your weaknesses or don't worry about them. It's — if you need to do this thing to achieve this goal that you have — make sure you understand what your goal is and then is this thing a thing you need to work on. For example, if you want to become a VP, you probably need to be really good in big important meetings and not be someone who just waits until everything's over and then shares an email of all your thoughts.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 对我来说，有一段时间我非常清楚自己不想被提拔——我在这个具体角色里非常开心，别打扰我。那条路需要的技能跟当管理者非常不同。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** That's right.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我很高兴你了解自己。因为很多年轻人进入职场后，所有人——可能整个家庭——都在告诉他们"你得升级，赚更多钱，拿到管理头衔，做到 VP"。但有时候人们不知道自己到底在签什么约——代价是什么？这真的是你想做的吗？和你的热情一致吗？我们当然有时候需要妥协。但我们可以设计自己的目标和路径。人们不开心往往是因为目标和实际不同步——想要大东西，但对达成那个东西需要做的事并不兴奋。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Um, yeah. For me, I actually went through a period where I was like, I do not want to get promoted. I'm so happy in this very specific role. Just like leave me alone. And that path is very different from the skills I need to build to be a manager. And then things change and then okay now these are the things I need to work on.

bilingual.zh: 如果你至少先知道自己理想中想做什么，然后告诉你的管理者，往往比你想象的更有可能实现。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah. I love that you knew that about yourself because I think it's so easy for — you know — a young person to go into their careers and everyone is telling them — maybe their whole family has been telling them — like you need to level up. You need to get paid more. You need to get that manager title. You need to get to VP. And at a certain point I think sometimes people opt into this without knowing what they're actually signing up for. Like what are the tradeoffs and is that really what you want to do? Does that really align with your passions? And of course, you know, sometimes we have to — again it's like it's a compromise for us, right? But we get to design what are our goals and what's the right pathway. Um and I go back to usually when people are unhappy it's because these things are a little bit out of sync. Like they want this big thing, but they're not actually excited about what it takes to do that thing and therefore it's just going to be a mismatch.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 完全同意。我们经常把管理者当成评判者——"他们会评判我做得好不好、该不该升职、该不该被开"。但在最好的关系中，管理者是向导。如果你理解管理者的工作——为团队产出更好的结果——也理解管理者眼中的成功是什么，你就能主动提出对团队有价值、同时适合自己技能的项目。
bilingual.en: And along those lines, uh it sounds like, oh, sure, I can design my life and design my role. But what I find is if you at least first of all know what you'd love and ideally do and then at least mention that to your manager, it often is a lot more possible than you think.

bilingual.zh: 反过来也一样——如果你问管理者"你的工作是什么？你认为成功是什么？你最大的希望和梦想是什么？"你就能帮助自己的职业发展。或者你说"这是我的希望和梦想，我觉得我擅长这些，我想提升沟通能力，如果你看到能帮我成长的机会请告诉我"。这样关系就变成了合作，而不是"我在让你给我升职，你在让我更努力工作"那种对抗。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** 100%. I think it's so important to be — like we often also have this mental model of like oh our managers are a judge and they're going to judge me on whether or not I got it — I did well — I should get a promotion — I should be fired. So there's this sometimes fear that people have. But I think in the very best relationships the manager is like a guide. It's like look the manager has a job and if you understand your manager's job which is how to get outcomes — better outcomes from the team — and also you understand what exactly would your manager consider success for the team? It also makes it easier for you to then be like, "Oh, well, if I do this project, then that clearly seems like it's a very direct path to creating value for the team." And that also is a kind of project that suits my skills. It's something I'm excited about. Like you should suggest that to your manager.

bilingual.zh:
**Lenny Rachitsky：** 有一篇 Ethan Evans 的客座文章叫"Magic Loop"，我会在节目说明里放链接，它是一个帮你弄清楚该做什么、怎么让管理者看到你的能力的框架。
**Julie Zhuo：** 说到管理者的永恒建议，特别是新管理者的建议，你已经分享了不少。还有什么你觉得特别重要、特别有趣、特别有价值的吗？
bilingual.en: But the other is true, right? So you would know that if you actually asked your manager, what is your job and what do you consider success to be and what is your greatest hopes and dreams? And then you might be able to help your own career and yourself um because you know that context. And conversely, if you say, "Hey manager, these are my hopes and dreams. This is what I think I'm good at. I really want to get better at this skill. You know, I really want to get that VP promotion, but I don't know what it entails. Can you tell me like what does it take?" That's a really wonderful conversation as well because then you'll get all of that context and then you can actually decide whether you want to do it or not. And if you want to then ask your manager for help, okay, if you see opportunities that are going to help me become a better presenter or increase my communication, please tell me. Even better, if you have feedback for me about communication, I want to hear it because that's what's going to help me grow in this particular skill. And so it becomes this collaborative relationship much more so than this almost like adversarial — like I'm trying to get you to give me a promotion and you're trying to get me to work harder. Like that is not a very good vibe.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 反馈是另一个我特别特别热衷的话题。我对自己以及所有共事过的人的总体印象是，我们对反馈的重视程度远远不够。公司都有绩效考核周期，大家就想着"好，每半年做一次评审"，到那时再接收反馈。但在我看来，反馈理想状态下应该是一种日常练习，因为决定团队长期竞争力的关键是——我们进步的速度有多快。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** There's a guest post by Ethan Evans that I'll link to that has a really good framework for how to actually do exactly what you're talking about called the Magic Loop where it's kind of a framework for figuring out what to work on and how to help your manager see you're capable of stuff and earn that trust.

bilingual.zh: 每周进步1%的团队和每月进步1%的团队相比，即便起点低很多，也会在很短时间内反超。那让我们进步最快的工具是什么？就是反馈。我对反馈的理解跟前面说的数据和指标很像——本质上是把你的假设拿去跟现实碰撞。举个例子，也许我现在觉得自己是个积极又有感染力的演讲者，我觉得自己在微笑、很有感染力、讲的故事很棒。但这是真的吗？我不知道。现实是我们经常有偏见，我们都知道这些心理学效应。比如 Dunning-Kruger 效应——人们觉得自己在某件事上比实际水平厉害得多。你问别人"你觉得自己开车水平高于平均吗？"70%到80%的人会说"是的，我高于平均"。这怎么可能呢？我们有偏见。而冒充者综合征是另一个方向的偏见——我觉得自己很差、不配待在这里。但这也是偏见，可能根本不是事实。实际上我可能完全配得上这个位置，别人也很看重我的贡献。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So along the lines of timeless manager, especially new manager advice, you've shared a bunch. Is there anything else that you think is really important, really interesting, valuable?

bilingual.zh: 所以我们对自己的认知——优势、弱点、正在发生什么——经常严重失准。要真正理解并持续进步，我们需要别人把他们看到的真相反映回来。我是这么理解的：这期播客结束后我会请你给我反馈，你会告诉我一些东西，而你给我的其实是一份礼物——把你看到的、我自己看不到的东西映射回来。就像我后脑勺粘了片树叶，我自己看不见。你告诉我"Julie，你头上有片叶子"，哇，谢谢，那我可以把叶子拿掉。反馈本质上就是这种映射，帮我们校准对现实的认知，让我知道自己是否在朝目标的方向前进。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Feedback is one of the other topics that I am super duper passionate about. And my general impression for both myself, everyone I've worked with is that we don't value feedback enough or we don't kind of think about it enough. Again, companies have these performance cycles and so we're all like, "Yes, every six months we're going to go and do these reviews." That's when I'll get feedback. But feedback really in my mind ideally should be like a daily practice because the thing that matters for us in the long run as a team is how quickly are we getting better.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我很喜欢这个比喻，完全同意。大多数人面临的挑战是——你也知道——怎么给反馈才能让对方真正接收而不产生防御心理，以及怎么接收反馈而不立刻想"他们根本不懂，他们凭什么这么说我"。能不能给我们一两个建议，怎么有效地给反馈、接收反馈，甚至主动寻求反馈？因为你说的都很有道理，但大多数时候人们根本得不到任何反馈。
bilingual.en: So a team that just gets 1% better every week compared to a team that gets 1% better a month is — even if they start off at a much lower baseline — is going to outperform in a very short amount of time the team that doesn't get better. And so what is the best tool for us to get better? It is feedback. And what I think about in feedback is it's very similar to what we said earlier about data and metrics. It's essentially trying to put your hypotheses and test them against reality. So as an example, maybe I have this perception right now that I am a positive and engaging speaker. So I have this sense that like I'm smiling and I'm very engaging and I'm telling great stories. But is that really true? I don't know. Like the reality is that I'm often biased and we all have, you know, we know these like psychological effects, right? We're like sometimes the Dunning Kruger effect — like people think they're way more expert at something than they actually are. You ask people, "Hey, are you better than average driver?" And it's like 70 or 80% of people like, "Yes, I'm better than average." How could that possibly be? We have biases. And imposter syndrome is a bias on the other side. It's like me feeling, "Oh, I suck. I don't actually belong here." Whereas that also is a bias. Like it may not actually be true. In fact, I might very well be here and other people value my contribution.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 最好的方法——第一个技巧是，在给反馈或传递困难反馈之前，先建立一种关系基础：我们认可彼此的贡献，我们希望帮助彼此成长，所以我们要成为每周都愿意互相给反馈的人。当你刚开始和一个人共事时，不要等到出了问题才给反馈，那已经是高压场景了。一开始就说："嘿，很高兴跟你合作。我觉得我们最好的协作方式是——我希望你帮我变得更好。我觉得我擅长这些，不太擅长那些。你呢？你觉得自己擅长什么、不擅长什么？我们就一起帮对方在这些方面进步吧。方式就是——所有反馈都是开放的，我希望你什么都告诉我。"理想情况下对方也会说"是的，我也希望你什么都告诉我"。这样关系基础就建立了。
bilingual.en: So we are just wildly out of sync a lot of times in our perceptions of ourselves, our strengths, our weaknesses, what's going on. And the way that we're going to understand and truly get better is we need other people to reflect back what is actually their truth. And the way I think about it is like I'm going to ask you for feedback after this podcast episode and you're going to tell me something and what you're going to do is you're going to give me a gift because it'll be a gift of reflecting something back of what you see that I can't see. Right? Just like if I have a leaf in the back of my head, I can't see that. And so if you tell me, hey, Julie, you have a leaf. Oh, wow. Thank you. Okay, maybe I can get rid of the leaf or whatnot. Um, but that is what feedback is. It is essentially reflection back. It helps us calibrate to reality and it allows me to get this information about whether or not um I'm moving in the direction of my goals.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这是同事之间，还是管理者和下属之间？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I love that. I completely agree. The challenge for most people, as you know, is giving feedback that people receive and don't feel defensive about and then receiving feedback and not being like, "Oh, no, they don't know. They don't know anything. How dare they say this about me?" Could you give us a maybe a tip or two for delivering feedback well and for receiving feedback well and maybe even just like seeking? How do you get more feedback? Because this all makes a lot of sense. Most of the time people don't get any feedback.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 所有人都适用。约会对象、孩子——任何人都可以。核心是先确定我们想要什么样的关系。我觉得大多数人都会选择一种可以亲近、可以坦诚说话、不用藏着掖着的关系。大多数人都会选择加入这种关系，一旦选择了，后面的一切都会容易得多。所以第一步就是让所有人都选择加入——这就是我们想要的关系。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** The best way — the first tip on getting feedback or delivering hard feedback is first go and actually establish that our relationship is one in which we value each other's contribution. We want to help each other grow and therefore we're going to be the kind of people that want to give feedback to each other every week. So when you first start working with someone, don't wait until something bad has happened. Now you have to give them feedback because that's already a pressurized situation. Start by saying, "Hey, really excited to work with you. I feel like our best collaboration is I want you to help me get better. I think I'm good at this stuff. I'm not so good at this stuff. What about you? Okay, you think you're good at this stuff. You're not. How about we just work together and we just help each other get better at these things? And the way we're going to do that is all feedback is open. I want you to tell me everything. Ideally, you're going to then say, 'Yeah, I want you to tell me everything.'" And we've already established that.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我听过一个很管用的小技巧，跟这个思路一致——问别人"你更喜欢当场收到反馈，还是每隔一段时间，比如每月或每周？"每个人都会说"当场就好，一有情况马上告诉我"。这就给了你自由，可以随时给反馈。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** And this is colleagues or manager or colleagues.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。如果你让大家都选择加入了——"是的，我希望我们关系好、互相帮助进步、我要反馈"——那后续传递困难反馈的难度，60%就已经解决了。第二个技巧是，在真正给反馈的时候，先检查一下自己的动机——我给这个反馈真的是出于帮助对方的初衷吗？如果答案是肯定的，那我们从60%又推进到了80%，大概率不会出问题。但经常发生的情况是，对方做了某件事触发了我，也许因为我以前有过类似的糟糕经历，我有点生气，想证明自己是对的。如果我给反馈的真正动机是想证明自己对、想告诉你你错了、想惩罚你——那不可能顺利。除非你是个极其出色的演员，否则不可能传递好。所以你必须先检查自己的意图。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** It's like everyone. It's like people you're dating. It's like, you know, your children — like it can be with everyone. Just establishing what kind of relationship do we want to have? I think most people want to opt into a relationship where you can be close, you can be tight with one another. You can say things to one another and not have to hide behind — I think most people will opt into it and if you opt into it, everything gets easier down the road. So the first thing is get everyone to opt in that like this is the kind of relationship that we want to have.

bilingual.zh: 但如果你检查过了——"不不不，我想过了，我现在很冷静，没有在气头上，我真的觉得 Lenny 只是没意识到他说那些话的时候会让我和其他人觉得被排除在外"——那我就需要把反馈说出来。这时候你可能会紧张，因为不想冒犯对方、很珍视这段关系、不想让对方产生防御。第三个技巧就是——把这种紧张直接说出来。比如我坐下来对你说："Lenny，我现在特别紧张——我想给你一些反馈，我很担心这会影响我们的关系，我非常珍惜我们的关系，不希望出任何问题，但我也觉得如果你能听到的话，对你是有帮助的。"
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** One trick throw out that uh I've heard that worked really well along these same lines is asking people — would you prefer feedback in the moment or do you prefer it kind of uh every once in a while, every month or every week or something like that — and everyone's like no no in the moment and just like tell me as soon as something happens and then that gives you that freedom to just okay yeah let me give you feedback here.

bilingual.zh: 这句话能完成大量工作——它是在展现人性。你会意识到我在冒险、在展现脆弱，你就更有可能真正听进去。比起我找一种方式把反馈"扔"过去，直接拥抱"这件事很难"这个事实，把它暴露出来，反而能建立更深的人际连接。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah. So if you get people to opt in — yes I want us to have a great relationship I want us to help each other get better I want feedback — that's 60% of the hard part of delivering difficult feedback later on. So then the second tactic I will say is that when you actually give the feedback, it helps a lot. First you have to check — am I actually giving this feedback because it's in the spirit of trying to help one another? And if the answer is yes, then we've like, you know, moved from 60% to 80%. It's going to go well, right? But what can often happen is I'm feeling like something happens, you do something, it triggers me because I don't know, I had like a bad experience about that type of thing before and I'm kind of feeling mad and I want to be right. Like if my real rationale for why I want to give you feedback is I want to validate myself. I want to be right. I want to tell you you're wrong. I want to punish you. It's not going to go well. There's no way you can deliver it and somehow — unless you're a tremendous actor — um it's just not going to go well. So, you have to first check your intention.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这是非常棒的建议，非常实操。好的，还有别的吗？我们聊了很多管理者的永恒智慧，尤其是新管理者需要听到的。你觉得还有什么是很重要但大家还没真正理解的？
bilingual.en: But if you've done that, you're like, "No, no, no. I thought about it. I'm calm now. I'm not seeing red. I really think that Lenny is just not aware that when he says this, it makes me and other people feel left out or whatever it is, right?" Then I need to be able to give it to you. And so usually then if you're like, "Okay, now I might be nervous because I don't want to offend you. I really value our relationship. How am I going to tell you — I don't want you to get defensive." What then the third tactic I have is just say that out loud. Like if I sit down with you and I say, "Lenny, I'm so nervous right now — I want to give you some feedback and I'm really worried that it's going to impact our relationship and I so value our relationship and I don't want that to happen, but I also feel like

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 双赢思维。这是我脑子里一直在转的一个概念。我觉得我们脑子里经常有一种叙事，觉得有些事情是对抗性的。
bilingual.en: it's just going to help you to hear it if you can." That does so much of the work — it's humanizing, right? It's like you're going to realize that I'm going out on a limb. I'm being really vulnerable and likely you're going to hear that so much more than if I just find a way to like drop it or lobby it over because it's so difficult. Just actually lean into the fact that it is difficult and expose that because that builds a lot of human connection.

bilingual.zh: 作为管理者，我想让人们更有生产力，我想让他们做他们可能不想做的事，我要让他们更努力工作，给他们施加更多压力。如果你开始这样想，这就不是双赢思维。这等于说我获得更好的结果必须以别人失去什么为代价。我觉得一旦这样想，就很难找到真正有效的策略。但如果你说："我的工作其实是找到双赢方案"——那我就不希望长期下来让某个人感觉我给他们制造了巨大压力，然后他们严重倦怠、辞职了，因为那对团队不好、对我不好、对长期关系不好。怎样才能找到双赢的解决方案？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** This is amazing advice. Very tactical. Okay. Uh is there anything else? So, we've talked about a bunch of timeless pieces of manager wisdom, things that people need to hear, especially as new managers. Is there anything else that you think is really important that people are just not fully getting for being great managers?

bilingual.zh: 这样想的话，很多事情都会变得更容易。比如新管理者——我自己也是这样——第一次不得不告诉某人"你不适合这个团队"的时候，内心极度煎熬。主要原因是我在代入对方的角色，想象这对他们来说是多么可怕，我做了一件天大的坏事。但还有另一种看法：如果这个人在团队里，他们大概也想成功、想做出好成果、想被重视、想发展事业。如果这里不适合他们——不匹配他们真正的兴趣和能帮助他们成功的东西——继续硬撑下去对这个人毫无好处。实际上只会很痛苦，等于在延长那种痛苦状态。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I think the idea of win-win, I think about that all the time in my mind. And I go back to because I think that often we have this story in our heads that sometimes things are adversarial.

bilingual.zh: 所以有时候双赢的做法就是直接说："看，现在行不通。我很尊重你、很看重你，我知道你想做一件让自己骄傲、能成长、被真正认可的事。而我们现在的情况不是那样的。"这是一种双赢的视角，而不是"开掉他们肯定是件可怕的事"。我不是说这不难，当然很难，但心态和思维模型会产生天壤之别——它会改变你传达这件事的方式，会改变在更大格局中这件事为什么可能是好事，也会减少那种对抗感——让对方不会把你看作敌人或权力掌控者。这必须是一种协作。如果不是双赢——我也可能是错的——我会说"我觉得现在不对"，对方可以说"不，你错了"，那反而是很好的信息，也许我们可以回过头来找到一种双赢的方式。
bilingual.en: As a manager, I'm trying to get people to be more productive. So, I'm trying to get them to do a thing that maybe they don't want to do. I'm going to try and get them to work harder or I'm going to um somehow put more pressure on them. Like, if you start thinking like that, that's not a win-win way to be thinking, right? That's like you saying my getting better outcomes has to come at the expense of somebody else losing something. Um, and I think if you start thinking like that, it's very difficult to come up with a strategy or to truly be successful. But if you say, "Look, actually my job is to figure out how to create win-wins." So, I actually don't want somebody over the long run to feel like what I've done is just create a ton of pressure for them and now they're super burnt out and they quit because that's not good for our team. That's not good for me. That's not good for our long-term relationship. How do we find the solution that can be win-win?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 是的，但他们必须真的相信这一点。你不能只是让它听起来像那样——"好消息！你被开了。这对你是巨大的胜利。"
bilingual.en: And I think if you think like that, a lot of things get easier. So, for example, with new managers, I think this is true for me too. The first time I had to tell someone that they're not — they shouldn't be a part of this team — was extremely fraught for me. And the main reason was because I'm putting myself in their shoes and I'm imagining that this is truly horrible and I've just done a huge disservice to this person and that's like the most awful thing. But there's another way to look at it which is hey if this person's on the team they probably want to be successful. They want to do great work. They want to be valued. They want to grow their career. If this is not the place for them because it doesn't align with their true interests and the things that are going to help them be successful — is just not the thing that they either want to do or can do. Um, at this point, it doesn't do that person any good for me to somehow try to continue to make it. It's actually going to be miserable. Like I'm going back to like prolonging that misery state.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 但实际上，你刚才那种说法几乎总是真的。这里就不是一个你会开心和成功的地方，去做别的事情更好。
bilingual.en: And so sometimes a win-win thing is to just say, "Look, it's not working. And I know I respect and value you so much that I know you want to do something that you can be proud of and you can grow in and that's going to be really valued. And right here, what we got, this isn't it." That's like a win-win way of looking at the situation, not a like — you know — oh my firing them is just definitely going to be horrible — I'm not trying to say it's not going to be hard. Obviously, it's hard, but it's in the mentality and the mental model, I think, makes all the difference because it's going to be different in the way that I convey it to them. It's going to be different in why this actually in the grander scheme of things may be great and it's going to reduce this adversarial feeling where they're now going to see me as like an enemy or somebody with all this power who's making choices that impact them and they feel powerless. It has to be a collaboration. And I think if it's not win-win — and I could be wrong — I would say, "I don't think it's right." The person could actually say, "No, you're wrong." And that would actually be great information because then maybe we can go back and we can find a way to make it win-win.

bilingual.zh:
**Lenny Rachitsky：** 是的。
**Julie Zhuo：** 好，我继续在这个池子里钓鱼看看还有什么，钓完了告诉我。你觉得还有什么是大家应该知道、应该听到的？特别是新管理者还没真正理解的？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yeah, I was just going to say they have to believe this. You can't just make it sound like this. Here's the win. You're getting let go. It's a huge win for you.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我觉得关注自己的能量和信念感非常非常重要。你会发现很多主题都回到同一个起点——你必须先理解自己、拥有正确的心态，做到了之后影响他人就会容易得多。这又是一个例子。我们谈了很多管理者的三大支柱，对吧？第一个是人——我们讲了维度观、反馈、帮助人们成长。第二个是目标感——我们来这里是做什么的？我们的北极星是什么？如果你自己都没有信念，就很难传递这种目标感。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Uh but in reality, the way you phrased it is actually almost always true. Like this is just not a place that you will be happy and succeed at, and it's better you go do something else.

bilingual.zh: 所以关注自己的信念非常重要，尤其是很多管理者一开始不是以创始人或CEO的身份进入的，你可能是个中层管理者。某种意义上你没有创造愿景，但被期待去执行它或承担其中一部分。我发现新管理者经常忽视的是自己真正的信念。他们觉得自己得像士兵一样，接到命令就执行。但如果他们自己经历过思考的过程——"等等，我们为什么要做这件事？我相信这个策略吗？它合理吗？"——如果不合理就去和上级对话，直到在"我真心相信我正在做的事"上达成一致——这真的会产生完全不同的效果。因为如果你不真心相信你在做的事，只是在鹦鹉学舌地传递组织层层传下来的东西，你就很难帮别人看到那种魔力，也很难成为一个有效的愿景承载者。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yeah.

bilingual.zh: 所以我觉得你必须真正地跟自己确认——"等等，我知道上面说要做这个，但我真的怎么看？"因为如果你感觉不对劲，项目大概率也不会成功。我可以告诉你，我管理过的每一个管理者，当他们说"我其实不觉得这是个好主意"的时候，我想不到有哪个项目最后出人意料地大获成功。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Okay. I'm going to keep fishing in this pool to see what else we got, but when we run out, let me know. Is there anything else that you think people should know, should hear, especially new managers that um they're still not fully getting?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这是管理者的经典难题——推进你并不真正认同的事情。我忍不住要问你——对于那些处在这种困境中的人，比如CEO优先推进某个功能，你觉得这不是个好主意，但你又需要表现出信心，不能让团队觉得你只是在传达命令、自己根本不信——你不想那样做，那样你会变成一个糟糕的管理者，人们会失去对你的信任。你对这些人有什么建议？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I think being aware of your own energy and conviction is really really important. So I go back — like a lot of these themes as you can see go back to like you have to first understand this about yourself and have the right mindset and when you do it becomes much easier to be able to be impactful with other people. So this is another one. I think it's very difficult for managers to be able — you know we talked a lot about the three pillars of what are the major um tools of a manager, right? The first is people. And so we talked a lot about like the importance of dimensionality and feedback and helping reflect and grow people. I think the second one is around purpose. Purpose is like what are we here to do? What's our northstar? And I think it's very hard to actually convey that if you don't have conviction yourself.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我觉得首先，如果你有这种感觉，你必须找到一种方式把它表达出来，参与对话。如果你觉得"我的上级让我做这个，我觉得是个糟糕的主意"——你必须去跟上级或CEO谈。因为一旦进入对话，往往会发生的是你会学到更多——新的信息、新的假设，也许你还能影响项目走向。关键是尽可能多地了解——为什么其他聪明人觉得应该做这件事？哪些部分我同意？哪些部分我持怀疑态度？你可以把它从"好或坏"的笼统判断拆解成——这是一个假设、那是一个假设、那又是一个假设。也许我相信这个假设，但我不喜欢这个提案是因为我不信某个特定假设，而其他几个我都信。
bilingual.en: And so watching your conviction is really important, particularly since a lot of people who are managers — you often start out not as like the founder and the CEO of the company but you might be like the middle manager. So in some ways you didn't like create the vision but you are in some ways expected to execute it or take a piece of it and do it. And I find that sometimes what new managers don't pay attention to enough is what is their true belief. Like they feel like they might have to be like a soldier. So they just get orders and they have to execute it. But it really makes a difference if they themselves have gone through the work of thinking through — wait why are we doing this? Do I believe this strategy? Does it make sense or not? And if it doesn't make sense to go and actually have the conversation with, you know, their manager or whoever else — just so they can get to alignment on like I really believe in what I'm doing. Because if you don't really believe in what you're doing and or you're just kind of parroting the thing that got passed through the organization, it's very hard for you to then be able to help other people see what that magic is or to be actually really effective as a person who can hold that vision and that purpose.

bilingual.zh: 当你能更深入一层地拆解成一组假设时，事情就容易多了——你很可能会找到某些你确实认同的部分，然后你可以引导方向。对于那个你不同意的假设，你可以 disagree and commit，但现在我们可以非常具体、隔离出那个东西。我们经常还可以这样做——"我不喜欢这个提案是因为我认为这个假设是错的。"举个简单的例子——你的提议是"我有一个好主意，我们要在每个街区开一个柠檬水摊"。而我的核心假设是人们不喜欢柠檬水，柠檬水不是当下的热门饮品。所以我觉得这是个蠢计划。但如果我跟你谈了，你说"不不不，这就是我们分歧的核心假设"，接下来很可能会展开的是——"好，那我们能不能找些数据？我们能不能先在一个市场测试一下，再决定要不要在全国50个州铺开柠檬水摊？"
bilingual.en: So, I just think you have to really check in with yourself on like, wait, I know we're told to do this and this is what we have to do, but how do we really feel about it? Because if you don't feel good about it, then it's not going to be very likely that the project's going to succeed. I can tell you right now, every single manager I've ever managed where they're like, I don't really think this is a good idea — there's no case where I can think of where the project somehow turned out to be like wildly successful.

bilingual.zh: 这样我们就能定位到具体的分歧点并找到解决方案。然后如果我需要跟团队说——"我们要测试这个假设。我自己对此也拿不准，但我觉得值得去验证。CEO 认为这是对的，我们就去做测试，用测试来回答——18到25岁的人在大学校园里是不是真的爱喝柠檬水？"这样就非常具体了，大家都会觉得"好，我也不确定，但我很乐意去测试并全力以赴"。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** This is such a classic challenge of managers — is getting things done that you don't really agree with. And uh I can't help but ask you for advice on someone who's in that place of just — okay we have this feature our CEO is prioritizing. I just — this is not a good idea — but I need to have a brave face and not make it sound like I'm just being told what to do and I'm just reporting orders. I don't believe in this. Like you don't want to do that. You become a terrible unsuccessful manager and people lose trust in you. What's your advice to folks that are in that place of just how to find that balance?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这个建议太好了。你还可以叠加一层——"这些是我确实同意和相信的部分，这些方面我认为完全正确；这一块我还不太确定，但这正是我们要做这个测试的原因。这是测试的最小版本，弄清楚答案本身就是个好主意。"说到这里，你的回答太精彩了，我几乎想再做一整期新节目，专门聊管理者的常见困境——每个管理者都会遇到的、当场很难处理的挑战。那个留到以后吧。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** So, I think first if you feel that way, you got to actually find a way to get it out and engage in dialogue. So, if you're like, I think my manager told me to do this, I think it's a terrible idea — you got to talk to your manager about it or you got to talk to the CEO or whoever — and feel because once you engage in a dialogue, what will often happen is you'll learn more. Like you'll have new information, you'll have new assumptions and maybe you'll have influenced a project in some manner. But often the more you can learn about — okay why did some other smart people feel like we should do this — and what parts of it do I believe and what parts am I more skeptical about — like you can probably decompose it from a blanket it's good or bad to like okay there's this hypothesis, this is a hypothesis, this is a hypothesis. I might kind of believe this one. The reason I don't like the proposal — I don't believe this particular hypothesis but I believe these other ones right.

bilingual.zh: 好，我们进入节目的几个固定环节——每期都会带嘉宾去的几个 Corner。首先是 AI Corner。我想问的是，你在工作或生活中发现了什么有趣或特别好用的 AI 应用？
bilingual.en: And so when you can start to get one level deeper into breaking it down into a set of assumptions that makes it much easier because then you'll likely find something that you do kind of resonate with and you might be able to then steer things like okay that hypothesis — you can disagree and commit — but now we can be very specific, we can isolate the thing. And what we can also often do is like okay the reason I didn't like this proposal is because I believe that this assumption is wrong, right? So — and they come up with like a really stupid example — but you know, your suggestion is I know we have a great idea. We're going to go and put a lemonade stand on every block. And my core assumption is people do not like lemonade. That's not the hot beverage right now. And so I therefore think this is a stupid plan. But if I talk to you about it and you're like, "No, no, this is the core assumption we disagree on." Likely what starts to unfold is like, "Well, let's just — can we get some data? Can we get some information? Like can we just — is there a quicker way to validate whether people like lemonade? Perhaps we should just test it in one market before we go and open up the lemonade stands across the entire 50 states."

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 关于学习和教育的部分前面已经讲了很多，我分享一个更好玩的故事。我家孩子们最近过生日，一个刚过，我二儿子的生日在两周后，女儿的生日也快到了。
bilingual.en: And so what happens is we can likely get to the actual specific area and come up with something. And then if I have to now share with my team — you know, we're going to try this hypothesis. I'm not sure how I feel about it, but I actually do think — like I don't know for sure. And you know, our CEO seems to think this is right but we're just going to test it and we're going to do the test in a way where that's what we want to find out — is like do people really love — like do 18 to 25 year olds love lemonade if we put them on these college campuses, right? So it becomes very specific and everyone's like well yeah I'm I don't know for sure but like I'm happy to go and test that and commit to it.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 顺便说一下，刚过去的是生日，不是孩子。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** This is such good advice. And there's also you could layer on — here's the things I do agree with and believe. Here's the ways that I see this as totally right. Here's the piece that I'm not so sure about, but that's why we're going to run this test. And here's why it's the smallest version of this test and why it's a great idea just to figure it out. And we'll show them. I mean, you probably don't want to say that. Uh, as you give this answer, it's so interesting. I almost want to do a whole new episode with you later of just like common conundrums managers have, challenges that every manager runs into that are really difficult to figure out on the spot. We could save that for the future.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 对，是生日刚过。是我孩子们的生日。今年我的一个目标是亲手给他们做点什么。回归 IC 身份，亲手造一个礼物。AI 让这件事变得特别有意思。我给最小的儿子——他六岁——做了一个东西，这个创意是从 Eric Antoniou 那里偷来的。你认识 Eric 吗？邀请他上过你的播客吗？
bilingual.en: Okay, I'm going to take us to a couple recurring uh themes on this podcast — occasional recurring corners that every episode uh we take guests to. The first is I want to take us to AI corner. And what I like to do in AI corner is ask what's a way that you've figured out to use AI in your work or your life that's just really interesting, really useful.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 还没有。我在努力邀请。他寄给我了那个——叫什么来着——methophone。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Well, I already shared a lot about education and learning, but I'll share maybe a more fun story. So, it's my kids' birthdays. One of them just passed and my middle son's birthday is in two weeks and my daughter's birthday is

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** Methophone。你看这个，不是在口袋里放手机，而是拿着这个东西走来走去，所有人都会问"那到底是什么鬼？"
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** by the way, the birthday just passed. The kid didn't pass.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 是的，我也是 methophone 的骄傲拥有者，下一版还有升级的小贴纸。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Okay. Yes, the birthday passed. That's right. That's right. The birthday passed. It's my kids' birthdays. And one of my goals this year was to try and build them something. So give them a present that has me going back to being the IC and making something for them. And AI makes this really fun. And so I just — for my youngest son who is six years old — this is an idea that I stole from Eric Antoniou. And um if if you know Eric, have you had him on your podcast?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** Eric 太棒了，你一定要请他上播客。他是个特别有创意的人。有一次我看到他肩上有只鹦鹉，我就问"你肩上为什么有只鹦鹉？"他说"你可以跟我的鹦鹉说话，这是一只会说话的鹦鹉。"然后我就跟鹦鹉说话了，鹦鹉还回话了。原来他在鹦鹉里面装了一个麦克风和扬声器，连接到了 ChatGPT 的语音模式。那只鹦鹉用海盗的声音说话。我当时就想"这是最棒的创意"。我六岁的儿子特别喜欢浣熊，家里有一大堆浣熊毛绒玩具。我就想给他做一只会说话的浣熊。所以我用 Eric Antoniou 的方法做了一只，效果非常好，他超级喜欢。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I haven't. I uh I'm trying to. He actually sent me — what is it called? — the methophone.

bilingual.zh: 现在我二儿子的生日快到了，他特别喜欢恶搞。他喜欢电子游戏，像 Minecraft，但他经常用 Alexa 听的是那些恶搞歌曲——就是把 Justin Bieber 的歌或者《江南Style》改了歌词，变成他在玩的某个游戏的恶搞版。那些歌唱得很糟糕，完全走调，就是某个人随便录的。我就想，既然他不介意走调的歌声，那我就给他做一张游戏恶搞歌曲专辑。所以我在 Replit 上做了一个应用——你输入一首歌，比如 Justin Bieber 的《Baby》，链接一首 Spotify 歌曲，然后给一些背景信息，比如 Lock 最近在玩 Kingdom Rush，我们有个关于石像鬼是"白送钱"的内部笑话之类的——我就给它一堆背景信息，让它写一首基于特定游戏的个性化恶搞歌词。它做得很好，质量相当高。然后它按照音乐的节拍来写歌词，然后我自己唱、录下来，就有一首歌了。所以我在制作一张专辑，准备送给他。他不会听到这期播客，所以别人不要剧透。我觉得发布的时候他生日已经过了。我对此超级兴奋。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Methophone. Check this out. It's like instead of holding the phone in your pocket, you hold this thing and then you walk around with it and everyone's like, "What the hell is that?"

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 等等，你要自己唱？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yeah. I too am the proud owner of a methophone and the next version upgrades with the little stickers.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。那首歌。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Eric is great. You should definitely have him on your podcast. He's such a creative character. Um and one time I saw him with a parrot on his shoulder and I was like, "Why do you have a parrot on your shoulder?" And he's like, "Well, you can talk to my parrot. It's a talking parrot." And then I spoke to the parrot. And the parrot spoke back to me. And what had happened is that he had hooked up a microphone. He like kind of surgically went into the parrot and added like a microphone and a speaker and connected it to uh voice mode on ChatGPT. So that — and it spoke in I think like a pirate voice. I was like, "This is the best idea." And like my six-year-old son is really into raccoons. He has like a huge amount of raccoon stuffies. I was like, I want a raccoon that can talk to him. So, I made that um using the Eric Antoniou method, but it was great. It was a huge hit.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 真的？
bilingual.en: And now my middle son's birthday is coming up and he is really into parody. Like, he loves video games, so Minecraft, but what he often listens to on his Alexa are these parody songs. So, it'll be like Justin Bieber's hit or like Gangnam style, but they've changed the lyrics so it becomes like a video game parody of some video game that he's playing. And they're horribly sung, you know, they're like off tune. It's just like some person who produced it. And I was like, well, if he doesn't seem to mind off-key singing, I'm going to create him an album of video game parody songs. So, I created an app on Replit uh that — what it does is you just give it a song like uh you know this is Justin Bieber's Baby and you link to a Spotify song and I give it some context like oh — Lock likes playing Kingdom Rush right now. We have like an inside joke about um you know the gargoyles being free money — like whatever it is I just give it a bunch of context like write me a song that just kind of personalizes it and it's a parody of this particular video game and it writes me the lyrics. It's pretty good at doing this. It's like pretty high quality. And then um and it — you know again it does it according to the beats of the music — and then I just sing it and record it and then I got myself a song. So I'm creating an album of this which I'm going to give to him. He's not going to hear this podcast so no one spoil it to him. I think it's going to publish after his uh birthday. But I'm very excited about this.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。我以为你会用 Suno 或什么 AI 工具来唱。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Wait, so you're going to be the one singing?

bilingual.zh: 不，我打算自己唱。AI 把一切都变得很简单，我只需要录就行了。我唱得不是很好，但他不介意——走调对他来说不是减分项。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yes. The song.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 哇，这太棒了——给了我好多给孩子们准备礼物的灵感。AI 在某些方面让做父母变得更容易、更有乐趣。这些例子都很棒。好，我们去另一个 Corner。Contrarian Corner。你有什么信念是大多数人会不同意的？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yes?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我相信每个方向都有无穷。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yes. I thought you were going to use Suno or some AI thing to actually sing it.

bilingual.zh: 这让我在几乎所有人说的任何事上都很逆向。当有人在 Twitter 上说什么，我有时会跟自己玩一个游戏——在什么情况下那其实不成立？我觉得现实世界是无限复杂的。所以比如我的孩子说"出去走走好无聊"或者"做某件事好无聊"，我的回答通常是——那是因为你没有看到那个方向上的无穷。即使是非常平凡的事情，比如盯着一面白墙。
bilingual.en: No, I think I'm going to sing it myself. Um and it just like all of this made it so easy. Like all I have to do is just read like record. And again, he's not — I'm not a very good singer, but he's not into — or he's not um it doesn't turn him off to have to hear off key.

bilingual.zh: 我觉得你可以让它变得非常非常有趣——你可以借此机会进入自己的内心世界，想办法让时间过去，或者冥想存在的意义、关注呼吸、感恩活着。同样是面对一面墙坐一个小时，一个人——像我的孩子——会疯狂抱怨说这是世界上最糟糕的事；但换一个人——比如一位僧侣——会获得一段美妙的体验。所以关键不在于环境或那面墙，而在于我们怎么看待它，能不能找到那个方向上深刻、丰富、无穷的东西。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Wow, that is so — this gave me so many ideas for gifts I can give to kids in my life. And I just love how AI is making it — I don't know — easier to be a parent in in some ways, more delightful. These are awesome examples. Okay, I'm going to take this to a different corner. Contrarian corner. What's something that you believe that most other people don't — most other people would disagree with?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 哇。这些回答很深刻。非常佛学、非常正念导向。我参加过一次禅修，他们的建议就是——任何时候觉得无聊，就注意周围正在发生的一切：你的座位现在是什么感觉？空气是什么感觉？你现在听到了什么？这跟你说的完全一样。有无穷的事物可以关注。但长期保持这种状态确实很难。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I believe that there's infinity in every direction.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 所以它才是一种修行。但我经常对自己重复这句话，因为有时候经历了不好的事，心情低落，这能帮我意识到——这大概是在我的脑子里。不是因为现实真的那么糟，而是因为我还没有获得看到其中丰富和无穷的能力。我可以在这方面努力。这种感觉比"我是环境的受害者，那件事发生在我身上太糟糕了，我无能为力"要好得多。后者对我来说是一种更差的感觉。另一种选择是——我只是还没掌握这项能力，我能认识到它是什么，我还没有，但我可以成长，可以变得更好。世界上有人和我处境相同，却比我积极得多，我难道不想成为更像那个人吗？
bilingual.en: So that makes me pretty contrarian on pretty much everything that anyone says. So if somebody says something like on Twitter, I sometimes play this game with myself, which is in what context would that actually not be true? And I think the reality is that the world is so — or at least my reality and my understanding of the reality is that the world is just infinitely complex. And so for example, if my kids say something like uh going outside is boring or taking a walk is boring or doing something is boring. My general response will be — well it's because you're not seeing the infinity that's in that direction. So even for example something really mundane like staring at a blank wall.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这跟我们第一期节目形成了完美的呼应——那一期我们聊了很多冒充者综合征以及你克服它的故事。我很喜欢这个。也许这是一个很好的结束方式。但在正式结束之前，在进入我们非常精彩的快问快答之前，你还有什么想补充或强调的吗？
bilingual.en: I think that you can make that actually deeply deeply interesting because you can use that as an opportunity to go into your own mind and to figure out like how you can make time pass or you can meditate on the existence or meditate on your breath or just be grateful for the purpose of being alive and like — two people, right? One person you can say sit in front of a wall for an hour and they will — like my kid — super complain and be like this is the worst thing ever. But you can put somebody else like a monk and they'll have a wonderful experience. And so it's not really about the environment or the wall. It's really about how we see it and whether we can find the thing that is deep and rich and infinite in that direction.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我只想说谢谢。说真的，你做的事情对我非常有启发。我们认识很久了，我觉得从你对这份 newsletter 和播客的最初构想开始，它已经变得非常了不起。世界从中获得了很多。我相信你经常听到这些话，但我真的很感激。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Wow. These are some deep answers. This is very uh I don't know — Buddhist — very mindfulness oriented. I did a retreat once and their advice was just yeah anytime you're bored just notice all the things that are going on around you like what does your seat feel like right now, what does the air feel like, you know, what are you hearing right now. And it's exactly what you're saying. There's infinite things to pay attention to and keep you interested. It's hard to actually do that for a long time and practice.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 非常感谢。我每次跟你聊天都会说——没有你，这一切不可能发生，Julie。你长期写的 newsletter《The Looking Glass》给了我灵感。我的想法本质上就是"如果我针对产品领域也做类似的事呢？"我像你一样从 Medium 起步，然后转到 Substack，然后想"要不收费试试？"结果成功了，然后"要不做播客试试？"也成功了。但这一切的起点都是你的概念。所以真心感谢你。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** That's why it's a practice. But I repeat that to myself because oftentimes if I have a bad experience I'm feeling a certain day. It helps me to realize that like it's often probably in my head. Like it's because I haven't gained the skills to be able to see the richness and infinity in that and that's — so I can maybe work on that and that feels better than feeling like oh I'm a victim of my circumstances. Like this thing happened to me and that's so awful but now I'm powerless. I can't do anything about — like that to me is a worse feeling than the alternative which is I just don't have the skill yet. I can recognize it for what it is. I don't have the skill yet, but I can grow. I can maybe get better at it. There is a person out there who had the same situation as me and feels much more positively than I do. And don't I want to be more like that person?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 而且你一直带着那么多善意和好奇心在做，我非常欣赏。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** That's such a beautiful circle back to our very first episode, which a lot of it was on imposter syndrome and overcoming that and your story there. So, I love that. That's a maybe a way to close this conversation. But before we do that and before we get to our very exciting lightning round, is there anything else that you wanted to mention or share or double down on that we've talked about?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这就是我这个人。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I just want to say thank you. Honestly, I'm so inspired by the work that you do. You know, we've known each other for quite a while. Um, and I just think from the very first idea that you had for this newsletter, for the podcast, it's been incredible and I think the world gets so much from it. I'm sure you hear that a lot, but I am very grateful.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 那么，我们进入激动人心的快问快答环节。我有五个问题，你准备好了吗？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Well, I really appreciate that and I say this every time we do a chat is just uh this wouldn't have been possible without you, Julie. Uh I was inspired by your longtime newsletter, The Looking Glass. That's essentially my idea was what if I do this for product and uh I started on Medium just like you did and then I moved to Substack and then it's like what if I charge for this and then that worked and then I'm like what if I do a podcast and then that worked but it all began with your your concept. So thank you truly.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 准备好了。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah, and I think you do it with so much kindness and curiosity as you always have. So, I love that.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 你最常推荐给别人的两三本书是什么？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** That's just who I am.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 第一本是《禅与摩托车维修艺术》。我太喜欢这本书了。写得很美，非常深刻。我关于"质量"的整套哲学很大程度上来自这本书。包括我们今天聊到的关于变化的话题——站在变化前沿、动态质量意味着什么——他在书中谈论得非常美妙和精到。虽然是老经典了，但我每隔几年都会重读一次。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Well, with that, we have reached our very exciting lightning round. I've got five questions for you. Are you ready?

bilingual.zh: 第二本是《Conscious Business》。这是我最喜欢的管理书。算是一本遗珠之作，因为我推荐这本书的次数实际上比推荐自己的书还多。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** I'm ready.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 哇。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** What are two or three books that you find yourself recommending most to other people?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我是在写完自己的书之后才读到这本的，我总跟人说，如果我在写书之前读过它，我都不确定自己还会不会写——因为《Conscious Business》真的太能引起共鸣了。我们今天聊到的很多东西——双赢理念、做一个参与者而非受害者、怎么看待工作不只是一份工作而是跟你的个人价值和世界观对齐——这本书讲得特别好。它也非常实操，有很多精彩的案例。我跟人说，这本书封面不太好看，乍一看很企业范儿。书名也有点让人摸不着头脑。第一章有点偏理论，但如果你能坚持到第二章，看到那个足球队的例子——就是最好的管理书。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** The first is Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance. I absolutely love that book. It's beautifully written. It's so deep. Um my whole philosophy around quality — it really comes a lot of it comes from that book. The idea and even all the stuff that we talked about — change — what does it mean to be at that forefront of change and dynamic quality. I think he just talks about it so beautifully and so masterfully in that book. So old classic but I try to reread it every few years or so.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这是很好的提醒，帮大家跨过那个门槛——找到这本书、看了封面之后能坚持读下去。
bilingual.en: Second is Conscious Business. It is my favorite management book. It's a little bit of a sleeper hit because I actually end up recommending this one far more than my own book.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 对。第三本。我很喜欢 Dr. Becky 写的《Good Inside》。这是一本育儿书，非常畅销。我推荐给所有父母。但我也觉得它是一本关于人际关系的优秀书籍，因为育儿本身就是一种非常深入和高强度的人际互动。我在育儿书里读到的很多东西，包括《Good Inside》，完全可以当作管理或团队领导的书来读。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Oh wow.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 我正在考虑邀请 Dr. Becky 上播客。正是因为这个原因，我觉得两者之间有很多共通之处。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I read this one after I wrote my book and I always tell people that if I read it before, I'm not sure I would have written my book because I would have been like conscious business is really the book that um really really so much resonates. And I think what — and many of the things I talked about — this idea of win-win, the idea of like being a player, not a victim, um and how to think about work, not just it's a job, but like how do you really think about aligning it with your own personal values and what you want to do in the world. I think that this book really speaks to that so beautifully. It is also very tactical. It's got a lot of really wonderful examples. Um, I will tell people the cover isn't very attractive and I think that if you judge a book by its cover, like this seems very corporate-y. The title also seems like kind of like what — conscious business — and the first chapter is a little bit more technical, but if you just could get past it and get into chapter 2 and you start, you know, with examples of the soccer team — it's just like the best management book.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 她用了"sturdy"这个词，我的"坚韧"概念可能就是从她那里来的——她大量谈论 sturdiness，这个概念直接植入了我的脑子。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** That is good uh good advice to get people over the hump when they look for it and they're like, "Okay, okay, I'm gonna stick with it."

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 是的。她的核心理念就是做一个 sturdy parent——坚强但有弹性。我太喜欢她和她的内容了，我会在 TikTok 上看她所有的视频，还有 Emily Oster 的。好，下一个问题。有没有你最近喜欢的电影或电视剧？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yes. And okay, third book. Um, I love the uh the book Good Inside by Dr. Becky. It's a parenting book and it's a very wildly popular parenting book. So, I really recommend it to all parents. But I also think it's just a wonderful book for thinking about relationships and because parenting is — it's like a very very deep and intense relationship and interaction that you have with another human being. And there's so many things that I read in parenting books including Good Inside by Dr. Becky that I think like could just as well have been like a management or a team leadership book.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我最近没怎么看东西——这个问题我没什么好答案。今年看的唯一一部是重看了《La La Land》，我真的很喜欢。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Uh I am thinking about trying to ask Dr. Becky to come on the podcast. I feel like there could be a lot of synergy exactly for that reason.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 太好看了。好，你最近发现什么特别喜欢的产品吗？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** And she uses this term sturdy which — I probably got it — I I mean I think she talks a lot about sturdiness and that just incepted right in here.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 没什么太新的。我喜欢 Granola。喜欢 Replit。各种代码工具都用过，目前 Cursor 用得最多。我刚入手了一台 Madic 扫地机器人，到目前为止体验很棒。至少设置过程非常令人愉悦——它有小贴纸，你可以把它装扮成狗或猫，非常好的体验。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Yes. Yeah. Her whole thing is creating — being a sturdy parent. Strong but flexible I imagine. Uh yeah, I love I love her and I love her stuff. I watch all her videos on TikTok and Emily Oster. Okay, next question. Uh is there a movie or TV show you enjoyed?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** Madic 机器人——我会放链接。我也是超级粉丝，我不是投资人。对不了解的人来说，它基本上就是 Waymo 版 Roomba——一个由 AI 视觉团队打造的非常高端的扫地机器人。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I have not watched anything — I have no good answer for you. I think the only thing I watched this year was a rewatch of La La Land, which I do truly love.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 对了，我还想到一个。Limitless 挂坠。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** So delightful. Okay. Is there a product you recently discovered that you really love?

bilingual.zh: 先声明，我是 Limitless 的小额投资人。但我喜欢它的原因是——它是一个挂坠，你戴着它，它会录下周围发生的一切，然后总结并给你反馈。我通常不在外面戴，因为可能会让别人觉得不自在。我一般会先征求对方同意。但我在家跟孩子们在一起的时候会戴。这个挂坠最棒的功能之一是——它会给我育儿反馈。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I don't think there's anything too new. I love Granola. I love Replit. I've used all of the different coding apps. Cursor is is big on me for now. Um, uh, I just got a Madic robot. I think that's been really delightful so far. At least the setup. I haven't used it like a long long term, but the setup, the way that it worked, the fact that it had little stickers and you could make it into a dog or a cat was like a wonderful experience.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 什么？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** The Madic robot — I'll bubble link to it. I am also a huge fan. I'm not an investor. That's uh essentially Waymo meets Roomba for folks that don't know anything about it. It's like a very sophisticated robot vacuum built by like AI vision people.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是自动的还是你把它扔进 ChatGPT？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Oh, I just thought of one more as well. Uh the Limitless pendant.

bilingual.zh: 不，它是自动的——有个app，有时会主动通知我，或者我去查看，也可以跟它互动提问。它本质上就像生活版的 Granola——捕捉一切、总结、给你建议和反馈。它说过这样的话："嘿，刚才你们在聊游戏的时候，你打断了孩子很多次，下次试试让他们把话说完再回应，可能会更好。"
bilingual.en: So disclaimer, I am an uh a small investor in Limitless. But uh what I love about it is that um so okay, it's a pendant. You wear it and it just records everything that's going on and later it summarizes things and it gives you feedback. And I don't usually wear it out because I find that maybe other people feel like awkward that I'm like recording everything. I usually try to get people's permission. But I do wear it at home when I'm with my kids. And one of the best things that the pendant does is it gives me feedback on parenting.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这个app本身就能做到？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** What?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。我不知道，因为我有一个但最近没怎么用。这太不可思议了。我想知道跟伴侣说话的时候它会不会也给关系建议。我好奇它怎么知道的。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Like automatically or do you throw it into ChatGPT?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 它的推断能力还不错，它识别出了那是什么样的人际关系，这对我来说挺让人大开眼界的。
bilingual.en: No, it will — it's automatic — like there's an app and it will sometimes notify me or if I check it, it'll — or I can also engage with like ask it. Um, but what it does is essentially it's like Granola but like for your life in terms of capturing everything, summarizing it and then giving you tips and feedback. And it said things like, "Hey, there was that time you were talking about the game and you cut your kid off a lot and maybe next time think about letting them speak fully and listening better."

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 太不可思议了。我们姊妹播客 How I AI 最近有一期——有人在跟CEO开会时戴着它，自动把会议内容转化成原型，然后销售团队可以拿去给客户演示看是否有兴趣。你觉得怎么样？
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** The app itself natively does that.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 太棒了。非常酷。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yeah. I did not know that because I have one. I haven't used it much recently. That is incredible. Yeah. I wonder if it gives you relationship advice too if you're talking to your partner. I wonder how it even knows.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 天哪。好，继续。你有没有什么人生格言是经常对自己说、或分享给别人的？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Um it's so — it was — it did a pretty good job of inferring you know that or I think it's a person too, but it was kind of eye opening for me.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我喜欢"Make it happen"。这是一个提醒——归根结底我们可以做很多事情，但我们也可能只是有很多动作。这让我想到另一句我很喜欢的话。Facebook 以前有一张海报写着："Don't mistake motion for progress"——不要把动作误认为进步。核心思想是——成为你想要的改变。换个说法就是——我能做事情，我们所有人都能做事情，我们有越来越好的工具去实现想做的事。Make it happen。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Incredible. So there's a recent episode of the How I AI podcast — our sister podcast — where somebody uh wears that in their meetings with their CEO and automatically turns what they're asking for into a prototype from the meeting notes and then sales teams can start showing it to people to see if they're interested. How about that?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 就像 Twitter 上那个流行的 meme——you can just do things。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** That's awesome. That is super cool.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 是的。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Holy moly. Okay, I'll keep going. Uh do you have a favorite life motto that you find yourself repeating to yourself, sharing with others?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 最后一个问题。我喜欢问那些深度参与 AI 的人这个问题。有没有什么是你在教孩子或鼓励孩子学习的——考虑到 AI 将在他们的生活中扮演重要角色？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** I like make it happen. Just a reminder that at the end of the day we can do a lot — we can have a lot of motion. Maybe this is another one that I really like. Um I think about this poster. It used to be a poster at Facebook that says don't mistake motion for progress. So there's this idea of like be the change you want to be in the world. I guess there's all other ways of saying the same thing which is like you know — I can do things. We can all do things. We have better and better tools to go out there and make things happen. Make it happen.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 情绪调节仍然非常非常非常重要。这可能是我在"希望孩子学什么"这个问题上想得最多的。我希望我的孩子能够自我审视，对自己的心理状态有更好的理解，因为我们仍然是人类。我们的硬件跟几千年前的人类一样，这一点不会因为工具和环境的变化而改变。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Like the common meme on Twitter, you can just do things.

bilingual.zh: 所以你必须真正了解自己——了解你内心正在发生什么、你在哪些方面有偏见、哪些方面没有。因为 AI 可以让一切变得更舒适——这是我最大的担忧。技术让事情更容易，这是我们发明的原因。人类一直在试图改善处境、掌控命运。但所有这些掌控到了某个点，我们的生活中有了太多捷径。你可以走关系的捷径，你可以绕过困难的情绪——因为你可以去刷 TikTok，而不是面对跟同事、伴侣或孩子之间的紧张关系。AI 让这种诱惑更大，因为现在有一个可以高度个性化的东西——你想要分心？想做点什么？它马上就有。但我们怎么才能继续学会面对真实的生物本性——那些不会改变的部分——怎么继续做那种愿意迎接挑战的人？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Yes.

bilingual.zh: 因为我发现如果我们不做有挑战性的事，我们会以另一种方式受苦。所以对我来说，真正的自由是——你可以选择困难的事，并为成为你想成为的人而自豪。这不是被迫的，不是为了生存，但你仍然必须做选择。我想让我的孩子明白——找到挑战仍然非常重要。你可以用 AI 来辅助，但不要把它当成一个走捷径的工具。因为如果那样的话，我觉得他们不太可能成为他们想要成为的那种人。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** Final question. I like to ask this question of folks that are really deep in AI and been working with AI and kind of getting a sense of where things are going. Is there something that you teach your kids or teaching your kids — think about encouraging them to learn — knowing that AI is going to be a big part of their life?

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 这是一个多么美好的方式来结束这次对话，Julie。感觉这就像是播客的某种里程碑——三年后你再次来到这里，就像旅程中的一个章节。感谢你回来，感谢你跟我们分享这些智慧。最后两个问题：大家在网上哪里可以找到你——如果想联系你聊聊 Sundial 或其他你在做的事——以及听众可以怎么帮到你？
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Emotional regulation is still really, really, really important. That's probably the thing that I think about the most in terms of what I want my kids to learn. I want my kids to be able to introspect, to have a better understanding of where their state of mind is because we're still human. We still have the same hardware that humans have had for thousands of years. And that's not changing even as the tools and the environment around us change.

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 我很希望跟那些在做很酷产品的公司合作，帮他们更好地构建产品。如果你觉得你的公司有兴趣跟我们 Sundial 合作，让每一个决策者都成为自己的专家分析师，请联系我们。邮箱是 sundial@sundial.so。我也在 X 上，最近发推更频繁了，分享各种思考——回到前面说的，练习把脑子里的东西分享出来。长文内容在我的博客 The Looking Glass，在 Substack 上，定期分享关于 AI、产品构建和领导力的文章。
bilingual.en: And so I feel that you have to really understand yourself and you have to understand what's going on for you and where you are biased and where you are not because AI can make it — and this is my great fear — is that it makes things so much more comfortable. And I have this great fear that this has been the trajectory that we've been on with technology right — this is again going back to like every strength is a weakness — technology makes things a lot easier, that's why we invent. Like we're always — the human race has always been about trying to better our circumstances and in some ways control our destiny like control our future. But at the same time, all of that control gets to a point where we have so many shortcuts in our lives and you can shortcut a lot of things. I can shortcut relationships. You can shortcut hard feelings because now you can just watch TikTok instead of actually dealing with the very difficult emotion or tension that you had with a colleague or with your partner or with your children. And AI makes it even I think more attractive because now there's a person who can — or there's a thing that can be very very personalized — and if you're like I want a distraction I want to do something you got that. But how do we actually still learn to sit with what is our true biology that's not changing and how do we continue to be the kind of people that want to take on the freedom of doing challenging things?

bilingual.zh: 还有我的书——修订版增加了两个新章节。一章是关于远程管理，另一章是关于在低谷期或困难变革中的管理。两周后出版。
bilingual.en: Because I find that if we don't do challenging things we just suffer — we suffer in a different way. And so to me, true freedom is you can pick the things that are hard and you can feel pride in becoming the thing that you want to be. It's not forced upon you. Like it's not for survival sake anymore, but you still have to pick. And I want to figure out for my children the fact that like it is really important to still find the challenge. Yes, you can use AI to do that, but really don't think about it as a shortcut tool. Um because if that's the case, I don't actually think that they're going to be able to become the kind of people they want to be in the world.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 新内容会在平装版中。这很重要——平装版有渐变封面。精装版最终也会更新，但从各个零售商换版需要时间。所以如果你买一本，我不能保证一定有新内容，但 Kindle 和平装版一定会有全部新内容。
bilingual.en: **Lenny Rachitsky:** What a beautiful way to end this conversation, Julie. It feels like this is just like some kind of huge milestone of this podcast, just like having you back three years later. It's like a I don't know, a chapter in the journey. Uh I appreciate you coming back. I appreciate you sharing all this wisdom with us. Two final questions. Where can folks find you online if they want to reach out and maybe chat about Sundial, maybe whatever else you're up to — and then how can listeners be useful to you?

bilingual.zh: **Julie Zhuo：** 那出版日期是什么？因为这期节目可能晚些才播出。
bilingual.en: **Julie Zhuo:** Well, I would love to work with people who are at companies building really cool things and want better answers to um how we build better. And so if you think your company would be interested in working with us at Sundial um and figuring out how do we make every single decision maker into their own expert analyst, please reach out. So that's one area. So, um, sundial at sundial.so. Uh, I am on X, so I've been tweeting a lot more, sharing, uh thoughts — you know, going back to that skill of practicing just share what's on your mind. But for the long form stuff, I have my blog, The Looking Glass. It's on Substack. Uh, I share articles and thoughts about AI, product building, leadership periodically.

bilingual.zh: **Lenny Rachitsky：** 9月9号。
